Miss-ing: Psychoanalysis 2.0, by Bruce Fink, 2024, Aeon Books, London, UK, £25.99, ISBN: -13: 978-1-80152-133-8

Lionel Bailly

Lionel Bailly

London, UK
l.bailly@ucl.ac.uk

Lionel Bailly  

This book is not so much a collection of papers as a book that did not know it had been written. It embodies the questions that Bruce Fink, as a psychoanalyst, has been pondering over the last 25 years, and when put together, the chapters demonstrate the coherence of his intellectual journey. In more than one way, this book reminds me of Freud’s Outline of Psychoanalysis, which seems to be the result of the reflection: what do I think others need to know and understand if they want to be psychoanalysts? In other words: it has taken me all my life to understand this, so you might as well benefit from it. Fink cares profoundly about psychoanalysis, and when he is asked to contribute a paper, he does it to the best of his abilities. This is not that common, and Lacan’s neological pun of “poubellification” for publication, which I might try to render in English as “pu-bin-ation” (or rubbish creation), has unfortunately remained meaningful, as the reading of some current psychiatric journals can confirm. Fink chose from the beginning to contribute to the development of Lacanian thinking, rather than to “know his place” and, instead, to devote his energy to producing commentaries upon commentaries on the original texts.

Regarding the book’s title, let’s note that on the first page, one finds the title Miss-ing. But something is indeed missing. On page 2, one can see the same title but this time it is followed 1267

Cynthia Gray

Cynthia Gray

2340 NW Thurman Street, Suite 202, Portland, OR 97210, USA
cynthiagray@gmail.com

Cynthia Gray  

This delightful collection of essays tells the story of a particularly generative chapter of our field’s history.

Beginning in the early 1960s, several forces collided to forge what is now the Psychoanalytic Center of California, a center for psychoanalytic training in the British object relations tradition. Some of the intersecting vectors included tribal hostilities at the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute (LAPSI), resulting in fractures and then re-groupings of analysts; seductively sunny weather that beckoned to analysts from colder climates to try a different way of living; and the fruitful connections made between one of the local splintered-off study groups and the London Kleinians who accepted invitations to visit and lecture. Wilfred Bion, Susanna Isaacs Elmhirst, and Albert Mason left England to settle and practice in Beverly Hills. Mason, to whom this book is dedicated, became the leading spokesperson for the British Kleinians in this area, helping to extend British object relations from Los Angeles to San Francisco, Portland, and Seattle.

This book gives the reader an intimate sense of what it was like to be in the actual company of these pioneer analysts. A hint of this is in the charming foreword by Barnet D. Malin, Arthur Malin’s son, who describes an evening in his family’s home where a cigar-smoking Hanna Segal gave him and his brother new miniature James Bond Aston Martin cars (complete with functioning ejector seats).

The experience in reading this collection is one of seeing “old friends,” and being immersed in their characteristic ways of speaking. For example, Segal’s coherent organization, which 450

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Extensions of Narcissism: Psychoanalysis, War, Climate**

E. César Merea

E. César Merea

Buenos Aires, Argentina
mereacesar@gmail.com

E. César Merea  

Proto Narcissism, Extensive psyche, Heteronymy

In this key note, five ideas - framework are presented to address the subject: the traumatic origin of the Psyche, as a distant echo of the Big Bang; the brain, mind, psyche trilogy, of different origins, but inextricably linked (mind and psyche are not synonymous); the idea of an extensive psyche, noun, not adjective, working between people and things; the extension of the Freudian topics to a 3rd and 4th inter subjective topics, and even a 5th, if the psyche is intervened by the technology; and the heteronymy of the Ego, composed of various psychic persons. From there, a proto-organic narcissism develops that is a defence against disintegration and also a model of subsequent functioning, both in war and the attack on the planet´s climate. Other issues, such as the power of the leader, the submission of the people, the relationship between psychosis and normality (not health), the “reasons of state”, and the ideology, are observed. An extensive psychoanalysis is proposed as a contribution to these painful issues.

[[RunningHead]]

Here I’m Alive. The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis, by Adam Blum, Peter Goldberg, and Michael Levin. New York: Columbia University Press, 2023, 304pp., ISBN: 9780231209441 (hard cover) 9780231209458 (trade paperback) 97800231557948 (e book)

Julie Nagel

Julie Nagel

Dexter, Michigan, USA
jjnagel@comcast.net

Julie Nagel  

Three collaborators, Adam Blum, Peter Goldberg, and Michael Levin, join the small chorus of psychoanalysts and philosophers who have attempted the formidable task of unlocking the importance, mystery, meaning, and value of music in our lives. The authors are listed in their biographies simply as members of the Bay Area psychoanalytic community.

648

Their book, Here I’m Alive: The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis, explores the polemic of music as the interface between being human in culture, thereby expanding a psychoanalytic view of human development that traditionally evolves from the coos and gurgles of the nursery into language. The writer-trio suggests that an internal and external interplay of various theoretical elements of music (i.e., rhythms, melodies, and harmonies) begins, inchoately, before birth and resonates throughout one’s lifetime, somatically and psychologically, to create what they call a “weave” or interplay between internal and external stimuli that they ascertain are inherently and fundamentally musical.

The authors’ approach to this topic reminds me of Erik Erikson’s 1950 classic “The Eight Stages of Man” that uses but expands Freud’s concept of stages. Erikson’s schema extends and conceptualizes development as life-long and adds societal and physical development to the psychology that evolves from the infant’s relationship with their caregivers. Erikson’s theory of development emphasizes, without naming music, the lifelong influence of infant sounds or

music (i.e., coos and gurgles) beginning in the nursery. According to the book’s authors, this music etween infant and caregivers becomes a prominent force that contributes to feelings of confidence, mastery, control, and relationships first with parents and then others not only through language but through sounds of the initial ohhs, ahs, gurgles, coos that are musicalized (emphasis added).

Here I’m Alive. The Spirit of Music in Psychoanalysis’s s parts with Erikson’s developmental theory, however, as they argue that the earliest sounds and interactions shape personality with the music of language evolving to the language of music. This theme has variations throughout the ten chapters of the book to emphasize that music allows one to feel alive and that this is relevant to being human and is underused in the clinical encounter. Regarding the latter, the authors assert that equal to or even more than words, the music of the body and tone of the voice (for both patient and analyst) can be heard and used by the analyst to respond to the patient in a way that adds to any verbal content exchanged in the analytic hour.

[[RunningHead]]

Israel–Palestine and the Internal World

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

Cambridge, UK
parsonstmc@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0009-0005-0365-1591

Michael Parsons  

Dear Editor,

Thank you for your “Psychoanalytic Controversy” section in a recent issue (vol. 106, 139–190). It seems valuable to ask how the Israel–Palestine conflict can be thought about from a psychoanalytic point of view.

Shmuel Ehrlich disagrees. He thinks the only question of psychoanalytic relevance is why discussion of the conflict is biased, as he sees it, against Israel. In his view, psychoanalysis has nothing to say regarding the conflict itself: “As psychoanalysts, we have no special expertise in understanding geopolitical, nationalistic or international conflicts. To pretend that we do is hubris” (Ehrlich, 2025, 168).

This viewpoint may be recognisable, but it leaves out something crucial. In a paper on authority and freedom, I wrote:

The conventional position seems to be that analysts may have their personal views concerning external authorities, political or of other kinds; as analysts, however, their business is the internal world, and opinions about external authorities are not psychoanalytic judgments. I do not agree. (Parsons, 2021, 1167)

The external world has profound impacts on the internal world, and this is something analysts do have special expertise in. In the same paper, I discuss at length the way a good external environment – benign government, peace, freedom, encouragement of individuality – nourishes the internal world, while a bad one – tyranny, war, demands for conformity, suppression of freedom – damages it. To think that analysts are not concerned with the external environment and therefore have nothing to say, as analysts, about it is not an analytic attitude. A more psychoanalytic stance is to ask whether what is done in the external world promotes inner, psychic growth or obstructs it, and then to support the former and oppose the latter.

Stephen Frosh expresses it well when he writes:

The failed environment … (the bombing in Gaza, the terrorist attacks in Israel – I am not talking about “equivalence” here, but about failures of care, renunciations of grievability, so that some lives cease to matter) … is one in which the conditions of necessary security are undermined, the social promise of continuity of responsive care is betrayed, and subjects (children, adults, people in general) are left with a hole in their inner world. (Frosh, 2025, 186)

[[RunningHead]]

Correspondence Covering the Psychoanalytic Controversies Section on the Israel–Palestine Conflict (Issue 1, 2025)

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

dbmossmd@gmail.com

Donald Moss  

Dear Editor,

We psychoanalysts have much to say about assertions of “reality” that are based on the putative authority of the first-person singular voice. When “my experience” is put forth as the last, and unimpeachable, word of an argument, the rest of us can only retreat, either in submission – you must be right – or in despair – you are out of reach. Neither reaction is a happy one, but we have scant maneuverability when the options presented to us come down to a crude two: you are right, you are wrong. The moment we even say, “but … ”, we are likely to be heard as insulting the foundational premise of the authority of that first-person singular voice. We are in the presence of a tyrannical epistemology. We, the second person in the argument, have no real place from which to claim a legitimate voice of our own. To speak is to persecute.

Shmuel Erlich (“Is psychoanalysis relevant to the Israel–Palestine conflict?”) writes in this self-exculpatory, self-authorizing, first-person singular voice. Listen. He says: “I have lived through” what I am talking about, from Kristallnacht” … and “I know from my own experience that the UN [United Nations] 1947 Partition … ” The entire text is grounded by what “I have lived through: and “from my own experience”. In this text, the central, indeed only, claim we hear from this voice is that anti-semitism is not only fuelling, but is, in fact, the basic premise of the harsh consensus underlying the worldwide critique of Israel’s operations in Gaza. Erlich even goes so far as to indict the International Journal of Psychoanalysis for its implicitly anti-semitic decision to place the Israel–Gaza question as a “controversial discussion”. Why not have controversial discussions about “Serbia–Kosovo, or Russia–Ukraine”, he wonders – as though, of course, there is only one answer to that question: the anti-semitic bias that treats Israel as the one indictable exception.

Let us imagine a Palestinian intellectual born in 1935, who was displaced in 1948, who has lived through 75 years of repeated displacements, repeated attacks. And let us imagine how this figure would begin his argument were he to be asked his opinion of Israel–Palestine. We can hear him, can’t we, as he answers “I have lived through what I am talking about … ”. And, in all likelihood, we would then listen with trained caution, alert to the distortions, the narrow viewpoint, of whatever might follow: his insistence on the premise of anti-Arab racism, the persistence of anti-democratic laws, the killings, the invasions. His appeal to personal experience, no matter how heartfelt, how precisely recalled, would simply not hold muster. Even his invocation of the Nacba – in Arabic, the “catastrophe – and its 700,000 displaced Palestinians”, would not be enough for us to entirely side with him. No matter how sympathetically we listened, we would respond with a crucial “but … ”. We would insist on context, on the force of international law, the United Nations, the world community, the Balfour Declaration, the shadow of the Holocaust. His first-person voice would simply not suffice – too limited, too parochial, too one-sided.

responseSet.title: Unmasking the Moral Fantasy of the “Underlying Conflict”: A Critique of Roberto D’Angelo’s “Do We Want to Know?”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unmasking the Moral Fantasy of the “Underlying Conflict”: A Critique of Roberto D’Angelo’s “Do We Want to Know?”

Oren Gozlan

Oren Gozlan

Toronto, Canada
ogozlan@gozlanpsych.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0782-5489

Oren Gozlan  

Roberto D’Angelo’s (2025) recent paper in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis reanimates an enduring fantasy in psychoanalysis: a wish for an “origin story” – a belief that the analyst’s task is to discover and treat the “underlying cause” of gender variance in youth. Beneath the ostensibly analytic posture of neutrality and concern, D’Angelo’s argument replays a tired suspicion towards trans identification – a suspicion framed as clinical precaution but tethered to an almost melancholic wish for psychoanalysis to return to its paternal function as guardian of the psyche against the distortions of culture.

However, what if the desire to locate an “underlying” conflict, to “treat” it before “affirming”1 (a term whose meaning is far from fixed) a trans identity, is itself a defence – one that veils the analyst’s own difficulty with the temporality of psychic life and the ethical demands of transference? As I have argued elsewhere, (Gozlan 2008, 2014, 2018, 2022, 2025) if we take seriously the Freudian (1905/1953) idea of Nachträglichkeit, or deferred action, then what we encounter in the analytic situation is never a stable core, never a clear “root cause”, but rather the plating out of dispersed fantasies whose origins are lost to time. There is no “underneath” to discover – only layers of displacement, repetition, and identification. The insistence on unveiling an “underlying trauma” or “unprocessed conflict” – whether anorexia, internalized homophobia, or a fragile ego structure – treats the symptom (in this case, transness) as a mistake to be reversed, rather than as a creative solution to the inassimilable.

There is a profound difference between theorizing gender as a defence against conflict and understanding gender as a way of working through it – a distinction D’Angelo collapses. Gender, like any symptom, is a mode of psychic labour. It is not that gender “masks” conflict, but that it recruits conflict, metabolizes it, and makes it available to psychic life. That this process may involve pain, or even a sense of urgency, does not mean it is illusory or pathological. Indeed, all psychic formations – cisgender, transgender, nonbinary, heterosexual, homosexual – are compromise formations in the Freudian sense. None are immune from displacement, and none are reducible to a single cause.

[[RunningHead]]

(In)security

Stephen Frosh

Stephen Frosh

UK
s.frosh@bbk.ac.uk

Stephen Frosh  

I first drafted this essay in the period immediately after the murderous Hamas attacks in Israel on 7 October 2023 and in the early stages of the ensuing devastating assault on Gaza by the Israeli military. A year later, there is still no resolution; things have moved on, but have only made the situation worse. In the wake of the ongoing and multiplying conflicts there have been thousands of deaths, even more thousands of injured, even more thousands of mourners. Bitter recriminations of all kinds have followed and are still resounding around Israel and Palestine and throughout the world; antisemitism, anti-Muslim and anti-Palestinian racism have escalated; and no-one – not Israel with its fortified borders, Palestinians in Gaza and the Occupied Territories, citizens of Lebanon or any of us anywhere else – is more secure. Indeed, the ongoing disaster simply spreads and people everywhere are affected. The words of the Hebrew-language poet Yehuda Amichai (1979) come to mind, from his poem “The Diameter of the Bomb”: “The diameter of the bomb was 30 centimeters”, the poem begins, but its effects radiate out to include the area where people are killed, the hospitals that deal with the wounded, the woman buried far away “in the city she came from” and the person who mourns her even farther away, which “includes the whole world in the circle”. The pursuit of violence makes everyone’s insecurity spread, creating waves of yet more insecurity and suffering, pursuing destruction, circulating it – the diameter of the bomb.

“Security” is the watchword of the Israeli state. Made insecure by the revelation that its vaunted intelligence and military machine could fail, as the Hamas terrorists flooded in and killed so many of its citizens and other people, its underlying insecurity produced an explosion of rage, a huge torrent of fury, the only aim of which seemed to be to destroy everything in its path. Is this where the obsession with security leads? One’s own security always at the expense of others’ – and, indeed, of one’s own in the end? Perhaps this is a variant of Jessica Benjamin’s (2017) “only one can live”, in which competitive victimhood becomes murderous. One’s own vulnerability rejects everyone else’s; it cannot even tolerate the comparison.

responseSet.title: But it’s Against the Rules!! Structured Competitive Games as a Neglected Resource in Child Psychodynamic Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

But it’s Against the Rules!! Structured Competitive Games as a Neglected Resource in Child Psychodynamic Psychotherapy

Celine Maroudas

Celine Maroudas

12a Hartzit St, Kiryat Tivon 3653214, Israel
celinems@live.com
http://orcid.org/0009-0008-2055-1068

Celine Maroudas  

Child psychoanalytic psychotherapy, board games, latency

In the field of child psychoanalytic psychotherapy, structured competitive games such as Monopoly, UNO or football have traditionally been regarded as less conducive to deep psychodynamically oriented work. By contrast, some contemporary authors have pointed out that in middle childhood it is often precisely in play with structured games – game-play – that spontaneity and strong emotions come to the fore. These authors suggest that game-play constitutes a potentially powerful therapeutic tool for access to, and communication with, the older child’s inner world. In this paper, clinical theoretical arguments are presented alongside clinical examples in support of this view and a variety of forms of game-play encountered in the analytic playroom are discussed and analysed. The paper examines how the rules and partially predetermined content of these games act as a framing structure in which the analytic work can take place safely and spontaneously, and how the model of the container ↔ contained can be usefully applied to game-play in the child therapy room. Emphasis is placed throughout on the therapeutic role of a flexible and carefully calibrated approach to the game’s rules and structure and the child’s cheating.

Transgenerational Haunting in Psychoanalysis. Toxic Errands by Maurice Apprey, Edited by William F. Cornell, 2024, Routledge, Oxon and New York, $39.95, ISBN: 978--032-48430-3, 236pp.

Marilyn Charles

Marilyn Charles

mcharlesphd@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1693-7207

Marilyn Charles  

In an era of conflict and unrest, Maurice Apprey’s new book, Transgenerational Haunting in Psychoanalysis. Toxic Errands, offers a welcome path towards considering what alienates us from self and others, and what moves towards healing. In contrast to current trends towards polarization, his is a conciliatory model that advocates for a recognition of common human values and ethics amidst differences of perspective. Collecting and expanding on his previous writing on transgenerational haunting, Apprey invites us to move into the spaces of greatest reactivity, both personally and collectively, while remaining open to possibilities of meaning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Couch of Paranoia – the Shadow of the Ancestors and a Haunting Ego Ideal

Hsueh-Mei Fan

Hsueh-Mei Fan

Taipei, Taiwan
mindspacefans@gmail.com

Hsueh-Mei Fan  

Ego ideal, the shadow of ancestors, paranoia

Based on her experience of working within two cultures, the author examines and explores how, in a Taiwanese context, one is encouraged to take on a different development route that emphasises maintaining identification with one's pre-Oedipal and Oedipal objects throughout one's life, in which separation and independence are discouraged and a familial-self is facilitated. This gives rise to a strong ego ideal that acquires a persecuting quality, and one could easily become prey to it. Clinical material is presented to illustrate this psychic situation, the plight it creates for the individual and how it manifests itself in the consultation room, turning the couch of understanding into a couch of paranoia.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Becoming Raced: Psychic Consequences of Transgenerational Racial Trauma

Dionne Powell

Dionne Powell

New York, NY, USA
dpownyc@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-7498-1644

New York, NY, USA

Dionne Powell  

Race, enigmatic messages, transgenerational trauma

Race, including racism, racial trauma, and racial anxieties, remains contentious for psychoanalysts to consider as a psychic phenomenon. Unlike aggression and sexuality, racism is held in silence and resistance, inhibiting exploration of this aspect of mind. This paper describes how we become raced by exploring the transgenerational transmission of racial trauma, writ large, and its intrapsychic, structuralizing components. By examining how and where the analyst locates themselves racially and working through our resistance and countertransference to this type of exploration, this paper invites us to include these aspects of mind for our analytic use. Using examples from the creative arts and clinical vignettes to demonstrate how being raced is embodied and symbolized in mind as a universal phenomenon opens a therapeutic aperture for clinicians who may benefit from a psychoanalytic, intrapsychic perspective on this aspect of our shared humanity.

responseSet.title: Lucy LaFarge (1948–2025): The Experience of Being Known
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lucy LaFarge (1948–2025): The Experience of Being Known

Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

New York, NY, USA
141 E 55th St, Ste 2F, New York, NY, USA
nkravis57@gmail.com

Nathan Kravis  

Lucy Bergson LaFarge, MD, was insistently herself. Coming from the time and place whence she sprang, that self-possession was hard-won. It was obvious from an early age that she was incredibly smart, yet that recognition was not exactly unproblematic. Nevertheless, she owned it. She read prodigiously. She inhaled literature. She brought her keen acumen to whatever came into her ken. All of us are the beneficiaries.

LaFarge grew up mostly in Cambridge, Massachusetts, attending Radcliffe College of Harvard University where her father was a distinguished economics professor. She attended the Albert Einstein College of Medicine and became chief resident of psychiatry there in 1978. She completed her psychoanalytic training at the Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research in 1988, where she went on to become a training and supervising analyst and served for 30 years as a highly regarded classroom teacher and sought-after analyst and supervisor. She attained the rank of Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Weill Medical College of Cornell University in 2009. She also taught and supervised at the Austen Riggs Center in Stockbridge, Massachusetts, for the last 10 years of her life.

At Columbia, Dr LaFarge was the recipient of the Beller Award (for a distinguished paper by a psychoanalytic candidate or recent graduate), the Klar Award (a teaching award bestowed by psychoanalytic candidates), the Daniels Award (the highest honour of the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine), the Goldman Award (the highest honour of the Center), and the Rado Lectureship (named after the institute’s founding director). No other person in the 80-year history of the Columbia Psychoanalytic Center and the Association for Psychoanalytic Medicine has collected all those honours. Columbia basically kept lavishing her with awards until it ran out of them.

There are several reasons for this unprecedented achievement, the most obvious of which was LaFarge’s sheer brilliance and productivity. A less heralded reason, but one that has been critically important to our intellectual community, was her generosity and generativity – as a clinical leader, in her unstinting presence as a classroom teacher and analytic supervisor, as a longtime course leader and curriculum designer, as an editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis where she served as Editor for North America from 2010 to 2020, and eventually as the first female editor-in-chief of The Psychoanalytic Quarterly.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Freud’s “Neurosis and psychosis” and “The loss of reality in neurosis and psychosis” 100 years later, edited by Gabriela Legorreta and Catalina Bronstein, 2024, Routledge, New York, 178pp

Miguel Leivi

Miguel Leivi

miguel.leivi@gmail.com

Miguel Leivi  

This book is part of the IPA series on Contemporary Freud Turning Points and Critical Issues, which has been published since 1991. It follows the main objective of the series: approaching aspects of Freud’s work from historical as well as from contemporary points of view. In this case, the selected works are the two short articles mentioned in the title of the volume, which were both published a century ago, in 1924. The focus of this book is on psychosis and reality, and the authors’ perspectives expose the complexity and diversity of psychoanalytic approaches to these topics. Eight psychoanalysts, belonging to different geographical regions and following diverse theoretical and clinical approaches, contributed to the book, alongside introductions to both of Freud’s essays written by the editors. There is a valuable trait common to all the articles: all of them include clinical illustrations of the authors’ ideas. I cannot do justice in this review to the varying perspectives put forth by these authors, but only take a bird’s eye view and focus in detail on a couple of examples.

Catalina Bronstein’s first introduction is a particularly useful guide to place these two papers within the context of the development of Freud’s works, especially regarding the intimately connected questions of what psychosis and reality are. Beginning with Neuro-psychoses of Defence, until his unfinished work on Splitting of the Ego, she talks about Freud’s evolving views on psychosis and reality. She also points out how subsequent authors such as Klein, Bion, Segal, Rosenfeld, etc. branched out from Freud.

Gabriela Legorreta’s second introduction deals with the articles of this volume, which take Freud’s ideas as a starting point, together with the contributions of many post-Freudian authors, including those men­tioned above plus others such as Searles, Aulagnier, Green, Winnicott, Giovacchini and Schaeffer, to develop their own ideas, proposals and emerging questions about the not-at-all-simple question of what is reality and the understanding and possible psychoanalytic treatment of psychotic cases, deemed impossible by Freud. The approach to the questions of the psychoanalytic treatment of psychoses is, as Matos de An­drade says, a constant challenge for psychoanalysis (15).

[[RunningHead]]

Traces of the Transplantation Complex in Jean-Luc Nancy's Body-Ontological Work**

Lutz Goetzmann , Marie Eichenlaub , Christian Benden , Annette Boehler , Adrian Siegel , Josef Jenewein , Annina Seiler , Uwe Wutzler and Barbara Ruettner

Barbara Ruettner

Maybachufer 20, Berlin 12047, Germany
goetzmann@ippk.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3260-897X

Luebeck, Germany

Hamburg, Germany

Zurich, Switzerland

Meilen, Switzerland

Zurich, Switzerland

Leipzig, Germany

Barbara Ruettner  

Transplant, transplantation complex, The Intruder, signification, body ontology

Lung transplantation is a complex psychodynamic process. In a prior study, the authors investigated 40 patients who had just undergone lung transplant operations. Using the analysis of a dream, they elaborated what they termed the “transplantation complex”. They also demonstrated that references are made to this transplantation complex in the further interviews of the total sample. In this paper, they examine whether aspects of the transplantation complex in the philosopher Jean-Luc Nancy’s essay “The Intruder”, in which he described his own heart transplant operation, can also be found in the eponymous work by the filmmaker Claire Denis. In fact, the themes of the transplantation complex appear both in Nancy’s essay and in Claire Denis’s film (such as fantasies that the donor is killed, that the recipient’s body is broken open and that the organs or the donor are devoured). The article closes with some explanations concerning Nancy’s “body ontology”.

Anna Maria Nicolò

Anna Maria Nicolò

anna.nicolo07@gmail.com

Anna Maria Nicolò  

This book, edited by Maria Adelaide Lupinacci, Nicolino Rossi, and Irene Ruggiero, originally explores the topic of reparation, a classical concept in Kleinian psychoanalysis, yet hitherto less thoroughly explored than it would have deserved. Of course, as the authors tell us in the Introduction, it is only natural for certain models or authors to be seemingly overshadowed in favour of others who take the leading role at that time. The most important concepts are thus absorbed or, by disappearing, become the foundations upon which later psychoanalysis is built.

The book consists of three parts: the first examines the psychoanalytic concept of reparation, its historical place and its development in the individual setting; the second part delves into reparation in work with children, couples and therapeutic groups; the third part studies the usefulness of reparation in relation to social trauma, genocide, groups of women 1074

responseSet.title: To Recognise Oneself
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To Recognise Oneself

Uta Karacaoğlan

Uta Karacaoğlan

Gutenbergstr 5550823, Cologne, Germany
karacaoglan@t-online.de

Uta Karacaoğlan  

Anonymisation, abstinence, disguise of clinical material

Publishing touches on fundamental questions of abstinence, it has an impact on our treatments. It is most likely impossible to prevent patients from recognising themselves (or wanting to recognise themselves) in clinical reports, but steps should be taken to prevent third parties from identifying them. My suggestion is to view anonymisation as a multi-stage process, in which the original clinical event is first documented and recorded in the form of a narrative based on the subjective experience of the analyst. This first step already constitutes an interpretation, as it usually involves a selection and summary of the clinical material. In a second step, a more general insight must be abstracted from this initial observation, one that applies not only to this specific individual case. Only after a structure has been identified does the actual anonymisation take place in the third step, in which the knowledge or hypothesis gained (about this structure) is illustrated in the form of a clinical vignette. This multi-stage process is illustrated with a clinical example. Whether this process provides sufficient protection against recognition by third parties presumably depends on the degree of acquaintance or intimacy between the analysand and the respective third party.

responseSet.title: From the Ambivalent Superego to the Impersonal Superego
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Ambivalent Superego to the Impersonal Superego**

Francis Pasche

Francis Pasche

Francis Pasche  

It [the supreme Cause] transcends all negation by the pre-eminence

of Its Simple and absolute nature – free from

every limitation and beyond them all.

(Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite)

The Superego is the Culmination of a Process Involving Multiple Factors

There is the capacity of the ego to “see” itself − which is not to be confused with the cogito defined by the intransitive sense of one’s own existence (Pasche 1991) − a capacity for standing back from itself, for becoming detached from itself, in order to apprehend both that of which it is the agent and the effect on it of what it receives and sometimes suffers (from the inside or the outside); in a word everything that envelops the cogito: sensations, perceptions, volitions, affects and judgements.

There is also, in a form that is difficult to imagine, and likewise innate, a sort of prefiguration of the external world but also the means of a pre-adaptation to this world and, particularly in Man, a pre-adaptation to his relations with his genitors or their substitutes: an archaic schema of mothers and fathers, of primal scenes, of the Oedipus complex, of castration, etc. It is primal phantasies that give parental images their supernatural dimensions. Freud (1923) rightly drew attention in The Ego and the Id (in the section on “The dependent relations of the ego”) to the phylogenetic roots of the superego.

Thirdly, fuelling the superego with energy that is always qualified (aggressive and libidinal), there is the id. An id with which the superego is in easier, more immediate, and more continuous communication than is the case between the id and the ego, which suppresses and represses the former to the extent of its strength. The superego will also be fuelled by the ego, and I will try to explain how.

Finally, there is parental reality; the parents strive by means of their instructions, refusals and prohibitions to infuse the child with educational elements of their own superego, but also love the child with tutelary love. They show the child tenderness, esteem and respect, whether they have received it from their own parents or not. This is the best opportunity for doing so. This love maintained at the level of the superego will become a constituent part of it, albeit external. We will see the havoc that is wreaked by a type of attachment between parents and children in which both are considered as equals. How is such a complex formation to be defined?

Denis Flynn

Denis Flynn

deniscflynn@yahoo.co.uk

Denis Flynn  

This is a series of essays recently delivered online as the A-Santamaría Association Bion Seminars by well-known Bionian analysts and scholars. Santamaría Linares invites us to read them in the light of the concept of caesura – as gateways into the many branches of Bion’s thought, by making contact with a “language of achievement” that can penetrate his ever “enlarging and evolving spiral of ideas” (2). She emphasises a core idea in the deep need of every human being for an experience of containment, to allow nurture and enable the growth of the mind. This approach makes containment central since it describes attempts to make contact in a communicative process between the mind of the patient and the analyst (1, 3). Many of these papers link Bion’s earlier and later thinking, most frequently here through the lens of his later thought and find new perspectives and insights to penetrate further into his ideas using his own developed later analytic approach – “without memory or desire”.

Santamaría Linares views the earlier and later Bion not simply in terms of one set of ideas “succeeding or conversing with one another” but as Bion himself would see it as different “vertices” (Bion 1970, 93), different perspectives of thinking that “give stereoscopic depth to one another” (7 emphasis added). Not all papers take this view, one or two distancing Bion’s work from his analytic roots in Klein and Freud’s thinking, so privileging or isolating later conceptualisations from his earlier thinking. It is clear to me, however, that Bion’s analytic thinking kept its roots in his earlier work and analytic influences although it did change over time, giving more full attention to the evolving unconscious aspects of both the patient’s and the analyst’s experience within the analytic process – the concept of “O” and Faith in O, F (O).

Viewing the earlier papers, Smith researches Bion’s autobiographical memoirs and focuses on how Bion’s early traumatic experiences as a boy and adolescent at school in England helped shape his attitudes and approaches to clinical work. Goldberg looks at Bion’s pivotal paper “On Arrogance”, linking Bion’s work with psychotic patients with the emergence of the theme of container – contained, ♀♂, which then led (I fully agree) to his theory of thinking and model of the mind. Goldberg sees Bion’s concept of “obstructive object” as “full of curiosity but at the same time [it] cannot stand to take in projections” so that it has the qualities of a primitive superego that augments the severity of splitting and fragmentation (38). Then a “psychotic process reverses the metabolising capacities of the mind replacing alpha function with a 1263

Jaap Ubbels

Jaap Ubbels

Noordeindeplein 4A, Leiden 2311 AH, The Netherlands
jubbels@xs4all.nl

Jaap Ubbels  

The Biography

A Dutch biography of the Jewish-German-Dutch psychoanalyst and literary writer Hans Keilson (1909–2011) has recently been written by Jos Versteegen. A German translation will be published shortly. Keilson is of iconic importance for post-war Dutch and German psychoanalysis. This is underpinned by his literary oeuvre in the German language consisting of a series of three novels (Life Goes On, A Comedy in Moll and The Death of the Adversary). It also includes several collections of poetry and essays.

The well-written biography is captivating because Hans Keilson was a man of many talents, whose colourful life has been marked by the dramatic European history of the first half of the twentieth century. He was able to sublimate in his work the destructiveness of which he was one of the countless victims. Yet he himself was deeply wounded, and this wound did not heal in his “new lives”.

Keilson grew up in a liberal middle-class Jewish family in a small town in the heart of former East Prussia. After the takeover of power by the Nazis, he barely managed to get his first novel published by S. Fischer in 1933. Then, a few years later he had to abandon his medical studies. As a qualified physical education teacher, he subsequently devoted himself to coaching Jewish children in Berlin. Following the introduction of the Nuremberg Race Laws, he fled to the Netherlands in 1936.

In 1939 he persuaded his parents to leave Germany to join him. Both parents were “abtransportiert” in 1943 and subsequently murdered in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Keilson himself survived the war by hiding. Although the existence of the gas chambers was known in the Netherlands by 1944, many remained hopeful that their loved ones would return. The biography aptly describes the dismay in 1945 when it became clear that this would not be the case.

Keilson remained in the Netherlands after the war. He qualified as a medical doctor, specializing as a psychiatrist and then as a psychoanalyst. As a (child and adolescent) psychiatrist, he was attached to the Jewish guardianship institution Le-Ezrad Ha-Jeled and was involved in many difficult and complex child custody cases of Jewish orphans.

responseSet.title: A Reading of Stereotypy in Autism Through the Concept of Iteration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Reading of Stereotypy in Autism Through the Concept of Iteration

Leandro Jofré

Leandro Jofré

LPCPP, Maison de la Recherche - bureau 143, 29 avenue Robert Schuman, EA 3278 Aix-en-Provence cédex 1 13621, France
leandro.jofre@outlook.fr
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2056-7042

Leandro Jofré  

Stereotypy, autism, iteration, invariable fixation, initial figure

Stereotypies currently occupy an important place in the clinical profile of autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Since they are usually described with the notions of sameness, resistance to change, or lack of variation, it would seem as if everything were repeated, and nothing could change. In this context, the following question arises: What is it that is repeated in stereotypical repetition? To answer this question, one must turn to clinical vignettes of patients diagnosed with ASD and to the concept of iteration stemming from two different epistemic fields (psychiatry and fractal geometry). Firstly, it is suggested that what is repeated in stereotypies, in particular, is the initial figure, since the elements that are unconnected to it change or may change. Secondly, specifically in the context of autism, it is suggested that what is repeated in a stereotypy is the very fact of repeating.

responseSet.title: Report of the Panel “A Three-Level Model Case Example: A Learning Experience Concerning Transformations”, 54th IPA Congress 2025, Lisbon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Report of the Panel “A Three-Level Model Case Example: A Learning Experience Concerning Transformations”, 54th IPA Congress 2025, Lisbon

Rui Oliveira

Rui Oliveira

http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8082-379X

Rui Oliveira  

This panel was organized by the Clinical Observational Committee of the IPA (https://www.ipa.world/Dev/Our_Org/Committees/CLINOBPC/CommitteeMaster?Code=CLIN+OB+PC), which has developed the Three-Level Model (3-LM) methodology for observing patient transformations (Bernardi 2015; Bernardi and Eidlin 2024, 2025). This method allows for in-depth clinical reflection on change and non-change in long psychoanalytic processes. It has been used as a powerful tool both for research (Rodríguez Quiroga et al. 2023) and for psychoanalytic training (Altmann de Litvan 2014; Hanly, de Litvan, and Bernardi 2021).

At an initial meeting, held online in February 2025, members of the IPA Clinical Observational Committee – including the panel presenters – discussed a clinical case (appropriately modified to guarantee confidentiality), applying the 3-LM methodology. The reflections, clinical discussions and conclusions presented by the speakers in this panel were “inspired” by the work of this group.

Mónica Eidlin, from Uruguay, presented the first panel paper, with the title “From Psychotherapy to Psychoanalysis”. She introduced an impressive clinical case of a young woman who, from the beginning, gave the impression that a “tornado had entered the office”: confusing, very distressed, pressuring and challenging the analyst. The carefully prepared clinical material referred to different phases of the treatment, highlighting mental functioning, the organization of the transference–countertransference relationship and the main transformative movements.

At the start, the patient’s emotional instability, agitation, intense anxiety, profound depression and risky, self-destructive behaviours (drug abuse, acting out) led the analyst to begin with transference-focused psychotherapy. Feeling somewhat powerless, the analyst witnessed the patient “playing with her own life”, condensed in her own expression as “trampling and not being trampled”, and also enacted in the transference.

Mónica highlighted critical moments for the deepening of the psychoanalytic process. For instance, after two months, facing intense instability and destructiveness both inside and outside the sessions, the analyst risked imposing limits on the setting – introduced as a gesture of care and support for the patient. This act became a fundamental organizing element, creating conditions to establish analytic work.

[[RunningHead]]

A simbolização na Psicanálise – Os processos de subjetivação e a dimensão estética da psicanálise [Symbolisation in Psychoanalysis – The processes of subjectivation and the aesthetic dimension of psychoanalysis], by Ruggero Levy, 2022. Editora Blucher: São Paulo. 338 pp, € 9,00, ISBN: 978-65-5506-442-1

Sergio Nick

Sergio Nick

Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
sergionick22@gmail.com

Sergio Nick  

Divided into three parts, this book studies the theme of symbolisation in psychoanalysis, its importance, and its evolution since Sigmund Freud. It then delves into the processes of subjectivation based on non-symbolisations, emptiness, and the development of the capacity for thought, within the most recent theoretical formulations. The author concludes the book by addressing adolescence as a momentum princeps for symbolic reorganisation, provided that the entire narcissistic dimension inherent to this period is taken into account.

In the first part, Levy provides a thorough overview of the topic of symbols, symbolisation, and the development of the capacity for thought. Beginning with the Freudian mental apparatus and the concept of representation, the author explores concepts such as the mnemic trace, symbols, thing-representation, and word-representation to show how the theme has been a part of psychoanalytic studies from the very beginning. When describing different approaches to symbols, the author distinguishes between the symbol as seen by Freud, characterised by its narrow sense, supra-individual, collective, and constant meaning, and Meltzer's interpretation, which emphasises a highly individual, personal, and idiosyncratic meaning. Nevertheless, by employing associative chains, the author demonstrates that the dynamic view was already present in the earliest stages of psychoanalysis.

For the reader, it is interesting to follow the description of symbolisation in Freud, Klein, and Bion, accomplished with clarity and detail. Levy’s explanation of the alpha screen, for instance, shows a complex concept in a simple manner, without omitting any essential details. By aligning this concept with the beta screen, Levy enables an understanding not only of the difference between the two but also how one transforms into the other. This entire explanation culminates in his assertion that a mother feeds her baby with both milk and “thoughts”, by putting the alpha function at his service. He adds that this is “much more than presenting the object of the baby’s need or desire”. I highlight this passage to underscore the author’s grasp of Bionian theory and its advancements over theories that have not managed to stress the importance of symbols, symbolisation, and the development of the capacity to think thoughts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intimacy and the Anxieties of Cinematic Flesh: Between Phenomenology and Psychoanalysis, by Patrick Fuery, New York, Bloomsbury Academic, 2023, 248 pp., $84.00, ISBN: 9781501376351

Nickolas Pappas

Nickolas Pappas

nickolaspappas60@gmail.com

Nickolas Pappas  

Intimacies and the Anxieties of Cinematic Flesh: Between Phenomenology and Psychoanalysis stages a conversation among phenomenology, psychoanalysis, and film, seeking the energy that dramas have when they grow beyond a mere pair of voices. Aristotle says it was Sophocles who brought a third actor into tragedy, and whether or not he did, we see for ourselves how Sophocles explored the possibilities latent in exchanges among three parties. One person speaks while the others respond in different ways to the speaker’s words.

Likewise, in this new book from Patrick Fuery, what could have remained an academic face-off between phenomenology and psychoanalysis keeps getting complicated by a cinematic scene that sends us back to the traditional disciplines to reconceive what they’d said.

Unlike such figures from tragedy as Electra and Clytemnestra, phenomenology and psychoanalysis as Fuery understands them do not need to remain at odds. He seeks resolutions in which the two approaches to human consciousness prove compatible with each other. Whereas the third party in Sophocles may bring news that crushes Electra while it calms Clytemnestra, cinema in its role as third participant in this book’s conversation offers experiences that mediate between the phenomenological and psychoanalytic conceptions of consciousness. It helps phenomenology and psychoanalysis each see what its fellow account of consciousness is thinking about.

The experiences that come from cinema concern intimacy and flesh. This book sometimes takes these terms concretely and sometimes interprets them most metaphorically, depending on the theme of any given chapter. After a first chapter that maps the terrain of the book with reference to Kierkegaard’s “Nothing” as occasion for anxiety, the remaining five situate intimacy and anxiety amid: the passage of time; space as externality; the “ir-Real” unconscious; wildness; the sexual nightmare. Each time, phenomenology (mostly in the person of Husserl, but also Merleau-Ponty) speaks first about the topic, then psychoanalysis (where that means Freud and Lacan, sometimes Klein, and more). Fuery outlines the junctures at which psychoanalysis diverges from phenomenology.

responseSet.title: Lucy Bergson LaFarge
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lucy Bergson LaFarge

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

I have never met anyone like Lucy LaFarge nor anyone who reminded me of her. Lucy was always irrepressibly herself and unplaceable to me, and these were some of the most appealing aspects of her.

I first met Lucy LaFarge in the early 1990s, when we were each invited to speak at a conference at Austen Riggs on working with the difficult-to-reach patient. A few years later, in 1995, we met again in, of all places, West Point Academy in upstate New York. It was on the seventy-fifth anniversary of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. Lucy was the first to speak and I felt an immediate sense of connexion to her when she stated: “I don’t like large groups of people I don’t know”. The comment, a mix of openness and suspicion, was so Lucy! She sought close, intimate conversation, probing to find those she could trust. When she felt trust in another person, her mirth, self-effacing humour, and generosity were on offer. This also came through in her very effective small working groups at IJP, the Psychoanalytic Quarterly, and Austen Riggs.

We learned that we had each that year been made training and supervising analysts at our respective institutes. We were each speaking at this anniversary celebration, and as I recall we quickly moved into a quite intimate conversation about our talks. We were young relative to most of the highly accomplished presenters whose papers we were asked to discuss.

In that same extended conversation, we discovered that we had in common that our fathers had been highly accomplished university professors. My sense in retrospect is that we were speaking of an evoked experience of being children with the grown-ups at a party, each feeling a bit out of place. Perhaps some sort of mistake had been made regarding our being given a platform to speak? It was a fun, surprising conversation. By the end of that conversation, I concluded that I had never met anyone quite like Lucy.

We also discovered that we had been reading each other’s writing, and we were both surprised and gratified by this fact. The first LaFarge paper that I read in 1989 was her brilliant paper on emptiness in severe regressive states. I was struck by its theoretical sophistication coupled with pragmatism and a lack of pretention in analytic work that I found quite useful. I knew that I would gravitate towards Lucy’s mind, and I sensed the same in return from her.

[[RunningHead]]

Bion in the consulting room: An implicit method of clinical inquiry, by Joseph Aguayo, R. D. Hinshelwood, Sira Dermen, and Nicola Abel-Hirsch, 2025, London and New York: Routledge, The Routledge Wilfred R. Bion Studies Book Series, Editor Howard B. Levine, MD, 164pp., ISBN: 978-1-032-50633-3

Carmen Mion

Carmen Mion

São Paulo, Brasil
carmenmion@uol.com.br

Carmen Mion  

Although Bion consistently emphasized that his ideas were deeply grounded in the challenges of clinical practice, he produced remarkably few written accounts of his own clinical cases or technical discussions on psychoanalytic methodology. Bion in the Consulting Room: An Implicit Method of Clinical Inquiry seeks to address this gap through a collaborative effort, initiated by Aguayo, to systematically study Bion’s clinical work in chronological sequence. This endeavour led to the formation of what the authors term an “Anglo-American research group” of British and American colleagues whose efforts culminated in the present book.

Recognizing that Bion rarely wrote explicitly about psychoanalytic technique, except in “Notes on Memory and Desire” (1967), the group’s inquiry centred on two key questions: “could Bion’s extant clinical work be studied and understood as its own separate domain, an implicit theory of practice”, and “Could a concentrated study of Bion’s analytic examples throw fresh light on his well-known theories”? (1). Bion’s clinical work and theoretical development were explored by the authors to determine whether he had a distinct analytic method.

As the group examined Bion’s clinical material, they employed the Implicit Method of Clinical Inquiry (IMCI) to describe his approach. IMCI, developed in 2015 and refined in 2017 by Dermen, encapsulates key elements of Bion’s thinking. It follows an inductive process in which the analyst begins with a set of preconceptions, carefully observes clinical material, and transforms these observations into conceptual formulations. This iterative process establishes a dynamic link between clinical observations and the evolution of psychoanalytic theory. The authors observed that Bion’s method informed both his technical interventions with patients and his supervisory work. Moreover, his clinical ideas often evolved into conceptual frameworks related to specific classes of patients. His theoretical models also contributed to the revision of fundamental psychoanalytic concepts originally formulated by Freud and Klein. In a moment of creative insight, the group characterized Bion’s IMCI as a “Janus-faced model”, encompassing both clinical technique and theoretical development (132).

responseSet.title: Structural Stability in the Analytic Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Structural Stability in the Analytic Process

Didier Houzel

Didier Houzel

Normandy, France
houzeld4@mail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-9348-3846

Paris, France

Didier Houzel  

Psychoanalytic process, structural stability, object-relations theory

The notion of stability is present in Freud's work. However, the libido theory model limited its use. Kleinian authors have explored this theme in more depth, describing the risks of the loss of ego stability or defensive systems protecting against chaos and mental turbulence. Dynamic systems theory has interested several psychoanalysts since the 1990s, as it seemed to offer a non-deterministic and non-linear model of the human mind. For some, it has a heuristic value as a metaphor for mental functioning; for others it offers the hope of a real formal modelling of metapsychological theories. The author focuses his attention on the forms of stability described in this theory: simple, periodic and structural. He puts forward the hypothesis of transitions from one type of stability to another in the psychoanalytic process, allowing access to the structural stability of representations that retain self-similarity. The analytic situation must offer an open dynamic system between two poles, maternal and paternal, which constitute the strange attractor necessary for the transition from a form of archaic stability to structural stability. Extracts from a child psychoanalysis are used by way of illustration.

responseSet.title: Processual Readings of the Drive Trajectory of a Perpetrator of Domestic Violence: Therapeutic Perspectives in the Prison Environment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Processual Readings of the Drive Trajectory of a Perpetrator of Domestic Violence: Therapeutic Perspectives in the Prison Environment

Elise Pelladeau and Alexandre Cedano

Alexandre Cedano

elise.pelladeau@univ-poitiers.fr

Alexandre Cedano  

Domestic violence, domination, prison, transference, drive, therapy

In this article the author looks at the psychotherapeutic treatment of a patient imprisoned for acts of domestic violence. She describes how the inversion of the drive coordinates (in this case, hypercathecting thrust to the detriment of the aim, which is normally to reduce tension) can herald a surge in mastery, which is then difficult to contain within the ramparts of the therapeutic setting. The sensory dimension at work in the transference is mobilized to illustrate how withstanding mastery can perceptually serve to rehabilitate the aim to the detriment of the thrust. This reorganization of the drive coordinates serves to lower tension against the backdrop of a struggle against the loss of an object whose contours are already very precarious. While violent acting out may indeed be seen as a failure of symbolization, therapeutic work in prison seems to maintain in this restricted space the hallucinatory trace of the lost/found object in the transference. Capturing the object would illusorily circumscribe the risk, but it is precisely through the handling of the meaning and strength of the transference that the patient can be enabled to let go, thereby freeing the lost object, or rather its ghost, in favour of the experience of loss.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Trauma of Racism: Lessons from the Therapeutic Encounter edited by Beverly J. Stoute and Michael Slevin, Abingdon, Oxon, and New York, Routledge, 2023, 394pp., £29.99, ISBN 978-1-032-24747-2

Sharon Numa

Sharon Numa

sharonnuma@gmail.com
sharonnuma@googlemail.com

Sharon Numa  

This book, skilfully edited by Stoute and Slevin, is a substantial collection of essays by American and British psychoanalysts and researchers. They examine racism in America from both historical and psychological perspectives, with its individual and collective consequences. Many are papers published elsewhere but they have been cohesively brought together here. The early chapters ground the work in an understanding of the socio-economic framework which continues to support slavery’s legacy. In the clinical discussions, the authors strongly argue for the need to acknowledge the social reality of African American lives in a dominant white culture weighed down and structured by this legacy. They track how the impact of racism has been theorised and explored in the literature, in critical theory and in psychoanalytic theory. Many essays implicitly or explicitly point to the conflict between examination versus denial and disavowal of racism. It is argued that the therapeutic endeavour must also grasp how this contradiction between knowing and not knowing might operate in the clinical situation.

The authors bring psychoanalytic theory and understanding to bear to detail the mechanisms that underpin racist thinking and behaviour, but the book also critiques psychoanalysis’s earlier reluctance to confront the issue of racism centrally, despite having the tools par excellence to do so. As Stoute notes, Freud pointed the way with his observation that people can be brought together in love, providing there are other people left to receive the manifestations of their aggression – but he did not pursue this theme explicitly in terms of the anti-semitism with which he was so familiar in mid-nineteenth and turn of the century Vienna (16). This book redresses the tardiness of psychoanalysis to address issues of race and does it well.

The central argument running through this collection is that racism in America constitutes a trauma – which Freud described as a breach in the psychic shield that overwhelms the ego – one that is constantly repeated through an accumulation of slights, insults, rejections and the experience of hopelessness. Although different aspects of racialised experience are examined across the five sections of the book, there is an overall coherence due to its psychoanalytic focus and its engaging vivid clinical and personal accounts. Many of the arguments presented elaborate issues raised in the seminal piece of work Black Psychoanalysts Speak, which was convened by Michael Moskowitz who observed the silence about the lack of black psychoanalysts.

responseSet.title: Tradition and Change in Psychoanalytic Theory: Querying the Infantile
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tradition and Change in Psychoanalytic Theory: Querying the Infantile

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

21 Vicksburg Street, San Francisco, CA 94114, USA
Stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu

San Francisco, USA

New York City, USA

Stephen Seligman  

Infantile sexuality, life and death instinct, Laplanche

Theory plays a crucial role in clinical choices, group formation, and organizational culture and politics. Theoretical positions seem based on reason and observation, but other motivations are often involved-belonging, recognition, and even concrete rewards, such as referrals and organizational positions. Established ideas can constrain new perspectives and observations that can enhance analytic theory and practice. I query the theory of infantile sexuality, critiquing several different theories' images of infancy: Freud's, Ego Psychology's; Melanie Klein's and Jean Laplanche's. I question both the analogy between infancy, psychic “primitivity,” and severe psychopathology (especially psychosis) and the idea that sexuality and aggression are the primary, endogenous motives. Rather than the essential “first causes” from the beginning of life, they are among an array of motives of interest to analysis. None of this precludes the core analytic ideas about the unconscious, primary process, object relations, fantasy, transference, and the salience of the inner world.

responseSet.title: Before and After the Fall: Horizontal and Vertical Object Orientations in the Analysis of a Patient with Grievances
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Before and After the Fall: Horizontal and Vertical Object Orientations in the Analysis of a Patient with Grievances

John Steiner

John Steiner

London, UK
johnsteiner28@gmail.com

John Steiner  

Revenge, grievance, super-ego

In this paper I describe how a vertical relationship of mutual grievance is commonly set up when the idealised horizontal relationship between infant and mother collapses. Mourning is interrupted if the lost object is internalised as a critical superego making the patient feel inferior and humiliated. He attempts then to turn the tables on the object in a response of manic triumph which creates the vertical, “up or down” world. This pattern is examined in clinical material which seemed to show progress even though the grievances were deeply entrenched. The reasons for the softening of his resentment were examined and the role of the vertical as well as other obstacles to mourning were reviewed.

responseSet.title: Dissociation, Repression, and Return. The Thirty-Year Arc of the Concept of Defence in Freud and Towards Contemporary Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dissociation, Repression, and Return. The Thirty-Year Arc of the Concept of Defence in Freud and Towards Contemporary Psychoanalysis**

Giuseppe Riefolo

Giuseppe Riefolo

Rome
giusepperiefolo1@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3496-5287

Giuseppe Riefolo  

Dissociations, psychoanalytic defences, Pierre Janet

The early Freud recognised a wide range of defences. Between 1894 and 1926, the only defence is repression. Breuer differentiates between splitting of the mind and splitting of consciousness. The former concerns the structure while the latter concerns the psychic content. Freud went on make dissociation coincide with repression by giving importance only to the splitting of consciousness, while for Breuer hysteria is founded on “splitting of the psyche.” In 1926 he proposed an enlargement of the field of defences in addition to repression with the recognition of particular defences in phobias and obsessions. This broadening retrieves Janet's concept of dissociation as ‘loss of the capacity for synthesis,’ which for the early Freud and Breuer occurred in hypnoid states and in the suspension of associative capacities. Contemporary psychoanalysis takes up Freud's initial concept of defences. It retrieves the concept of dissociation considered both as repression and as the inability to integrate experience caused by blocking the fluid dissociative dynamic between multiple states of the Self. Using clinical examples it is therefore proposed that the analytic process simultaneously includes positions of repression as well as positions of dissociation considered as suspension of the patient's and analyst's integrative capacities. Repression concerns the avoidance of conflict, while dissociation suspends the process of access to conflict.

responseSet.title: Editorial: “All Goodness is Dispersed”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: “All Goodness is Dispersed”

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

London, UK
fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

Few psychoanalysts will have remained untouched by the war and the terrible situation in the Middle East involving Israelis and Palestinians. It is already catastrophic, its potential consequences even more so. But within our psychoanalytic world, the war “out there” has also triggered bitter conflict and strife “in here”, meaning within our professional community. Erstwhile friendly colleagues find themselves pitted furiously against each other. Psychoanalytic institutions have come under fire from different directions, often simultaneously, and have been quite unsure of what is for the best in current circumstances.

Given that war could be seen as the ultimate example of acting out, one could be forgiven for presuming that psychoanalysts would all be against such destructive action. Yet analysts have taken up positions, finding fault with one side for taking such action, or defending the other side for its aggressive resistance – even though each position involves the taking of lives. And within the talking, wordy world of psychoanalysis, protagonists have not withheld their verbal punches against those colleagues whom they are quite sure are their enemies. Ordinary doubt seems to have given way all too often to hostile certainty.

It is this completely unusual context that has led Elizabeth Allison, the editor of the IJP Controversies section, and me to commission a series of papers by some within the analytic field who are well placed through their positions and their experiences to reflect psychoanalytically on what is happening out there, and in here. This is not a complete departure from our norm: our readers will know that from time to time we publish papers on political situations. Regarding the Ukrainian war, we commissioned two Letters from Kyiv and Moscow, and we recently published an Education Section on War, with reflections on the horrors of our contemporary world. As we do so often, we base ourselves on the practice of our founder: throughout his life, Freud wrote about external situations as much as internal ones, including his papers on war. But, as already stated, it has been particularly the nearly internecine conflict within psychoanalysis that clinched the decision to publish a series of papers on a subject about which I am quite certain our readers will already be highly exercised and troubled. “Controversy” is an understatement for the sheer power of conflicting thoughts and feelings that have been stirred up by this crisis. I should add that these papers were written towards the end of 2024, during the height of the Israel - Gaza conflict. Since then, there have been some developments towards peace, though most commentators are agreed that the underlying conflicts are far from being resolved.

responseSet.title: Letter from Dresden
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Dresden

Kai von Klitzing

Kai von Klitzing

Leipzig, Germany
kai.vonklitzing@uniklinik-leipzig.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3010-5121

Kai von Klitzing  

Training, politics, history of psychoanalysis, war, Freud

In light of the upcoming conference of the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF) in Dresden, this letter deals with the eventful history of psychoanalysis in East Germany, especially in the state of Saxony. After a first heyday between 1923 and 1933, marked above all by the early clinical and scientific work of Therese Benedek, the development came to a sudden end with the seizure of power by the Nazis and could not be revived after the Second World War under the communist regime. This dark period of history clearly demonstrated that psychoanalysis can only flourish under social conditions of freedom. While psychoanalytic thought is slowly recovering from its decline during the two German dictatorships, psychoanalysts are facing the dark clouds of the resurgence of antiliberal and intolerant political movements that threaten liberal democracy in Europe and other parts of the free world.

responseSet.title: Living an Experience of Defence: Exploring Defensive Process within the Play Framework
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Living an Experience of Defence: Exploring Defensive Process within the Play Framework

Steven Cooper PHD

Steven Cooper

275 Central Park West, 1C, New York, NY 10024
USA
Stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

Defense, play, paradox

The author deconstructs how elements of defense analysis operate during the process of play in analytic work. In play, a patient’s defense process often involves an experience of aliveness and vividness within the analytic couple and is less oriented toward secondary process metacommentary about defensive function than is typical of more formal defense interpretation. The emergence of the analysis of defense that occurs during play is not meant to challenge other forms of defense analysis but is rather adjacent to these forms of defense analysis. This view of a cluster of approaches to defense analysis is consistent with his view that epistemological and ontological approaches often operate simultaneously in analytic work. The author theorizes defense process during play and clinically examines these processes in two vignettes.

responseSet.title: Doubts about Ending Analysis and the Internal Parental Couple
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Doubts about Ending Analysis and the Internal Parental Couple

Lesley Steyn

Lesley Steyn

London, UK
lesley.steyn@talk21.com

London, UK

Lesley Steyn  

Termination, parental couple, countertransference

Describing countertransference feelings and conflicts towards end of treatment is difficult for analysts and the literature on termination tends to focus on what should happen not what does. The inherent incompleteness and inevitable frustrations of patient’s and analyst’s wishes can throw both into confusion at the very point when the wish for certainty and completion is heightened. This paper describes a case where patient and analyst repeatedly felt thrown into doubt about whether and how analysis should end. The analyst came to think that, unconsciously, the doubts related to the patient’s position with the internal parental couple. As the patient struggled with feelings of disintegration and exclusion the analyst experienced periods of theoretical confusion, which stood for her relationship with her analytic parents. The analyst came to see these periods of disintegration, situated within the transference-countertransference field, as crucial to the developmental process, pushing the analytic couple away from what could have become false “depressive” coherence towards more truthful encounters. She views the patient’s capacity to bear this as a sign of readiness for termination. Retrospectively she finds helpful light can be thrown on this experience by Britton’s concept of post-depressive paranoid- schizoid position, synthesizing Bion’s extension of Kleinian theory and Steiner’s concept of pathological organisations, but she places more emphasis than Britton does on describing the countertransference struggle. Ultimately, the author argues, the patient can be helped to achieve fuller integration if the analyst can tolerate some disintegration, repeatedly losing and regaining an internal parental couple.

responseSet.title: A Butterfly Painting its Own Colours: Enactment, Visualisation and Differentiation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Butterfly Painting its Own Colours: Enactment, Visualisation and Differentiation**

Sebastian Kohon

Sebastian Kohon

80 Engadine Street, London SW18 5DA
Laufer House, London, UK
skohon@londontherapygroup.co.uk

Sebastian Kohon  

Reverie, visualisation, enactment, somatic countertransference, adolescence, après-coup

The clinician’s emotional and somatic responses to patients are an essential source of information, but how can we disentangle what belongs to whom? A certain blurring of boundaries is inevitable in psychoanalytic work, with oscillations between more and less differentiated states of mind↔body, patient↔clinician and patient–clinician dyad↔wider institutional setting. In one sense, psychoanalytic work can be conceived as a cycle of repeated regressive enactments, followed by elaboration and differentiation après-coup. Referring to two clinical vignettes with pubertal/young adolescent patients, I reflect on the role of the clinician’s visual imagination in this process. Accessing an internal, dimensional space can re-establish a boundary, while simultaneously processing a communication, thus forming a bridge between somatic reaction and thought. The particular relevance of this in relation to working with young adolescent patients is considered.

responseSet.title: Do we Want to Know?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Do we Want to Know?

Roberto D’Angelo

Roberto D’Angelo

Los Angeles
roberto@robertodangelo.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2929-3831

Roberto D’Angelo  

Transgender, LGBT, psychoanalysis, transition, detransition

The weak evidence base and profound consequences of gender-affirming interventions for youth call for a particularly sensitive and complex psychoanalytic exploration. However, prohibitions on knowing at the individual and social levels significantly constrain psychoanalytic work with trans-identified youth. Barriers to exploration and thinking that patients bring to treatment are reinforced and reified by the dominant socio-political trends that saturate the contexts in which young people dwell. These trends increasingly frame any attempt to deeply explore why a young person is seeking medical or surgical gender-affirming interventions as “off-limits” and a form of conversion therapy. Furthermore, politically driven clinicians who promote medical gender-affirming interventions misrepresent and attempt to discredit clinicians who explore the meaning and function of trans identification, or who express concern that transitioning may be a drastic solution to various forms of psychic pain. In doing so, they minimise the significance of the weak evidence base for these interventions and their serious, known risks. At the same time, they obscure or deny the psychic pain that is sometimes humming beneath the experience of gender dysphoria. The author asks: If there are significant uncertainties and risks of harm associated with medical interventions for young people, do we want to know?

Lucio Gutiérrez  

Digital presence, immersion, online psychoanalysis, screen, teleanalysis, virtual

The concepts of immersion and digital presence have been widely used to describe the experience of being intensely engaged and the blurring of the differences between tangible and digital environments (e.g. being “in” the video game or “with” the other person on the screen). Existing theories have tended to explain these from models based on adaptation to the environment, evolutionary automatisms or social construction of the world. Alternatively, a psychoanalytic reading of these concepts will be proposed, including the problem of cathexis, defence, the negative work of the psyche, and the dynamic equilibria involved. This theory will be illustrated by applying it to teleanalysis, video gaming, and to some aspects of social networking. We shall also speculate about states of mind in immersion, psychopathologies of virtuality and some of the risks that digital immersion poses to primitive and early emotional development. Finally, the value of considering immersion and digital presence within the psychoanalytic research agenda on human relations in and with the virtual-digital is raised.
