responseSet.title: Thinking Under Fire: What can Psychoanalysis Contribute to Understanding Aspects of the Israel–Palestine Conflict and our Feelings about it?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thinking Under Fire: What can Psychoanalysis Contribute to Understanding Aspects of the Israel–Palestine Conflict and our Feelings about it?

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

London, UK

London, UK

1-19 Torrington Place, London WC1E 7HB, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

Elizabeth Allison  

In war the enemy’s object is so to terrify you that you cannot think clearly, while your object is to continue to think clearly no matter how adverse or frightening the situation. The underlying idea is that thinking clearly is more conducive to being aware of “reality”, to assessing properly what is real. But being aware of reality may involve being aware of the unpleasant because reality is not necessarily pleasing or welcome. (Bion, “Making the Best of a Bad Job”)

The last Psychoanalytic Controversies section in this journal explored some of the contributions that psychoanalysts might make to thinking through the challenges that humanity faces today (Allison 2024). However, while some of the contributors (e.g. Frank 2024; Rustin 2024) touched on the exceptional challenge to our capacity to think represented by the situation that has developed in Gaza and its sequelae involving other groups across the Middle East following the events of 7 October 2023, this latest manifestation of the longstanding Israel–Palestine conflict was not the primary focus. This is because we came to realise that in order to have any chance at all of doing this particular topic justice, it would require its own space. We are taking the risk of approaching it in this current Controversy with considerable trepidation, mindful of the extraordinary intensity of the feelings that are stirred up both by our own and others’ efforts to engage with it. Many if not most psychoanalytic societies will have had painful recent experiences of this emotional turbulence and the polarisation it can generate. And yet looking away is not an option. Although he was not writing about the most recent developments in the Israel–Palestine conflict, Fakhry Davids captured the dilemma vividly in his reflections on another recent episode:

As the airwaves filled with sounds and images of that brutal war (Rothchild 2014) – of bombings and buildings being reduced to dust, of streets filled with the blood of the dead and wounded and the agony, pain, distress, grief and rage of the survivors – and of politicians fighting a propaganda war, it became just about impossible to think. At a time like that how could one turn off the news in order to write about Palestine-Israel? Yet, how can one have the news on and think? (Davids 2016, 42)

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and the Israel–Palestine War: Perspectives on our Relevance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Israel–Palestine War: Perspectives on our Relevance

Harriet Wolfe

Harriet Wolfe

2105 Divisadero St, San Francisco, CA 94115
USA
president@ipa.world

Harriet Wolfe  

Polarization, projection, Levinasian ethics

The relevance of psychoanalysis to geopolitical matters of war and large group suffering is explored. The presence of polarization within small and large psychoanalytic collectives is seen as an effort to manage clinicians' sense of helplessness in the face of overwhelming human suffering. The impact on the clinician of the inability to alleviate suffering that is occurring on a massive level, the difficulty in remaining empathic when the suffering of the other is unimaginable, and the use of projection to maintain internal coherence are considered. The ethical foundation of psychoanalysis defined by Levinas as responsibility for the other is highlighted as essential to restoring cohesion within the profession. A ceasefire within the profession is posited as an essential step toward a restored ability to think together, engage productively with one another, and determine a constructive role for psychoanalysis in the currently violent sociopolitical surround.

responseSet.title: Is psychoanalysis relevant to the Israeli–Palestinian conflict?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is psychoanalysis relevant to the Israeli–Palestinian conflict?

Shmuel Erlich

Shmuel Erlich

13 Disraeli Street, Talbieh, P.O.Box 4356 Jerusalem 91042, Israel
shmuelerlich@gmail.com

Mount Scopus Campus, Jerusalem 9190501, Israel

Shmuel Erlich  

An item in the New York Times this morning caught my eye. Featuring a picture of a woman attired in a shawl that seems to represent the colors of the Palestinian flag, it read:

This year’s Turner Prize, the prestigious British art award, was awarded to Jasleen Kaur, an artist whose recent installation work focuses on her childhood growing up in a Sikh community in Scotland.

A small but noisy pro-Palestinian protest took place outside Tate Britain, the art museum in London where the prize ceremony was held, and Kaur, on accepting the award, called for Tate to end ties with Israel. “It’s not a radical demand—this should not risk an artist’s career or safety,” she said, adding: “Free Palestine.”

Since the Hamas attacks on Israel in October 2023, many artists have signed letters calling Israel’s retaliation in Gaza a genocide, while Jewish and Israeli donors have often felt threatened by the level of vitriol directed toward them. (NYT, 5/12/24)

This item represents in a nutshell the dilemma of dealing with this issue and joining this discussion. Assuming for a moment a naive position, which I obviously do not own, it makes you wonder: What is the relevance of the Israeli–Palestinian conflict to this prestigious event, recognizing an artistic achievement? Why is there a pro-Palestinian demonstration outside? What is the connection, if any, of this artist, raised in a small Sikh community in England, to the Middle East and its turbulence? And why, in accepting her prestigious prize, does she end her acceptance speech with the call, “Free Palestine”? To underline and round off the significance of this cameo event, we are told that she is not alone in this, and that many artists have expressed their critical view of Israel’s “genocide” in Gaza.

Indeed, this small item is representative of the current emotional upheaval that surrounds this conflict. Many of those responding to the crisis from afar are influenced by the written and televised press and social media, which in many cases supply distorted and biased information. Others, like for example the students demonstrating in the universities, often display ignorance about basic facts, such as which river and what sea they refer to in the slogans they shout. Listening to the abounding lectures and televised debates, I find that often the speakers either have scant knowledge of the facts or choose to severely distort them.

responseSet.title: Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, 22 February 1936–8 August 2024
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, 22 February 1936–8 August 2024

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Mark.Solms@uct.ac.za

Mark Solms  

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis – training and supervising analyst in the German Psychoanalytic Association – was the preeminent Freud scholar of her generation, second only to James Strachey in the history of Freud scholarship. Her enormous contribution was formally acknowledged when the Sigourney Award was bestowed upon her in 1998 – and in the same year, when she won the Sigmund Freud Prize for Academic Prose from the Deutsche Akademie für Sprache und Dichtung.

Despite her considerable accomplishments, she avoided the limelight, and seldom spoke about herself. Some of the details I will convey here are, therefore, probably unknown to most of her colleagues. They draw mainly from a public interview which was conducted by her close friend Hermann Schultz, in the form of a “Meet the Analyst” session at the Chicago congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association in 2009. In many places I will parapharase what Ilse herself told Hermann Schultz about her fascinating life and work.

She was a “lay” analyst, deeply steeped in the humanities. She was imbued with historical methods and with texts, images and symbolic systems. This, rather than medicine or natural science, was her point of contact with psychoanalysis. She made fundamental contributions to the scholarly study and publication of Freud’s writings, helping to reintroduce his ideas to the German-speaking world after the major breach caused by the Nazi regime.

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis graduated from the “Hochschule für Gestaltung” in Ulm (where she was born): a then newly founded academy of design, visual communication and information science. This academy no longer exists, but it became legendary. She studied there from 1955 to 1959, at a time when the German nation was just beginning to confront the full extent of the horrors that were perpetrated in its name during the Nazi era. The founder of the Hochschule in Ulm was Inge Aicher-Scholl, whose two siblings were executed in 1943 by the Nazis, due to their participation in a student resistance group, Die weiße Rose. The school was conceptualized as a successor institution to the famous Bauhaus schools of Weimar and Dessau (which were dissolved by the Nazis in 1933), some of whose members returned from exile to lecture in Ulm – including Josef Albers and Walter Gropius.

responseSet.title: Bion. An Introduction, by Nicola Abel-Hirsch, 2023, Phoenix Press, London, £17.99, ISBN: 9781912691838
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion. An Introduction, by Nicola Abel-Hirsch, 2023, Phoenix Press, London, £17.99, ISBN: 9781912691838

Michael Halton

Michael Halton

mi.halton@btinternet.com

Michael Halton  

Since Bion’s death in 1979 there have been many books and papers on his work, and second to Freud, he is the most frequently cited author in psychoanalytic publications. To my knowledge, the first introductory text to Bion was published in 1975 (Gringberg et al.). Unfortunately, it was a rather compacted work that mostly reiterated and only marginally clarified Bion’s own dense and obscure style of writing. Fortunately, by contrast Nicola Abel-Hirsch’s recent contribution Bion: An Introduction has few of these faults, and we are given a clear and comprehensive account that is both clarifying and easy to read. The author received much praise for an earlier publication, Bion: 365 Quotes (2019), which cleverly selected extracts of Bion’s writings, one for each day of the year. Each selection could then be read as if it were a poem or thought for the day and the book could be perused in bite-sized chunks. Reading Bion demands patience and a mental preparedness that rewards slow, careful attention. It is to Abel-Hirsch’s credit that she cleverly recognised this fact and gave us a Bion-a-day quote for careful meditation and reflection. I have profitably used these quotations for teaching seminars, and the format has been a stimulus for focused and rich discussion. Abel-Hirsch’s new book, Bion: An Introduction, on the other hand takes a different tack and is a valuable companion that views the scope of Bion’s work as a unified whole.

Early in his psychoanalytic career Bion wanted to submerge himself deeply in clinical experience to get a long and hard look at the basics and founding principles, perhaps akin to the effort made in physics to discover the irreducible elements of subatomic particles. For this reason, his writing, with the exception of the straightforwardly autobiographical work, is often difficult and highly abstracted, sometimes irritatingly gnomic and arcane, but nearly always challenging. Considerable effort is required on the part of the reader to bring Bion’s abstractions into contact with one’s own lived experience whether personal or clinical. One finds oneself having to read and reread his sentences, pausing to think, masticate and search for concrete examples and realisations of his ideas that match one’s own experience and knowledge. During his career Bion gave very few clinical illustrations, and, where he did, they were often highly condensed. Similarly, he was spare in his references to other psychoanalytic authors.

Sull’arroganza. Saggio di psicoanalisi [On Arrogance: A Psychoanalytic Essay], by Giuseppe Civitarese, 2022, Jaca Book, Milano, 204 pp, €22,00, ISBN: 978-88-16-41776-2

David Ventura

David Ventura

Via Monte Tomatico 1, Rome 00141, Italy
david.ventura@spiweb.it

David Ventura  

At the 20th Congress of the International Psychoanalytic Association (IPA) in Paris, Wilfred Bion read a brief paper entitled “On Arrogance”. Although over time it became one of his most famous articles (1958), psychoanalysis has rarely felt the need to go back to it and consider its subject, and never in the breadth that Civitarese devotes to it in this essay. This is quite surprising when we think that it was through these very reflections on arrogance that Bion was prompted, in the Oedipus myth, to shift the interpretative centre of gravity away from sexuality to the relationship that man establishes with the truth. And it is perhaps all the more surprising if we consider that, in emphasising the importance of that paper for the development of his theories, he himself drew attention to its unexpressed potential (Bion [1967] 1984). These ideas were perhaps too dazzling for the era when they were conceived, some of them falling into an oblivion of the kind lamented by Tiresias who, after warning Oedipus, saying “Alas, knowing is a terrible thing if it does not help him who knows!” applies the warning to himself: “I knew this before I came: but I had forgotten it” (Sophocles 2009, 33).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Braving the Erotic Field in the Psychoanalytic Treatment of Children and Adolescents, by Mary T. Brady, New York, Routledge, 2022, 235 pp., £28.79, ISBN: 9781032210025

Antònia Grimalt

Antònia Grimalt

74 Roger de LLúria Street, 08009 Barcelona, Spain
agestelrich@hotmail.com

Antònia Grimalt  

The changing body of the adolescent patient presents particular challenges to containment and pressures towards enactment. The bodies of patient and analyst are more directly the subjects of analysis with adolescents than with adults or with younger children. Adolescents need help to name and integrate their newfound bodily experiences. Their minds need to grow into the bodies they now inhabit. It is a challenge for the analyst to talk about feelings the adolescent has barely begun to name: the area of erotic transference/countertransference in the treatment of adolescents is largely ignored in analytic writing. The editor’s musings about the book title drive her to the term “braving” as this process does, indeed, require bravery as the kinds of powerful feelings the erotic dimensions tend to evoke may be potentially difficult to navigate.

Brady offers us a frank view of the erotic dimensions of clinical psychoanalysis with young patients, which she and her contributors discuss. “Erotic” includes more than sex; it also encompasses: “harmony, relatedness, purpose, significance, and meaning: This means, of course, that ambivalence, anxiety, jealousy, rivalry, and a sense of lack will also be present” (2). 213

responseSet.title: Upgrade to PI.4, or Rethinking Projective Identification from the Vertex of the Analytic Field
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Upgrade to PI.4, or Rethinking Projective Identification from the Vertex of the Analytic Field**

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Via Teodorico 8, Pavia 27100, Italia
gcivitarese@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Projective identification, intercorporeality, Bion, relationship (♀♂), ontology of the flesh, Merleau-Ponty

The paper attempts to answer a key question: does the concept of projective identification (PI) still have a place in the metapsychology of post-Bionian analytic field theory (AFT)? Over time, PI has been characterized as increasingly interpersonal, especially as revised by Bion and Ogden. Beginning with the Barangers, writers on AFT have also spoken about what might best be called “crossing” PI as the foundation of the analytic field. However, on the one hand, they have understood this intersection negatively, as the basis of paired bastions or collusions; on the other hand, with Bion, they have always maintained a certain unidirectionality in writing that the flow of PI normally goes from the patient to the analyst, and can only occasionally be reversed. Strictly speaking, this notion clashes with the radically intersubjective idea that every fact of analysis is unconsciously co-created and represents a “character” in the analytic field or an affective hologram of the couple. The author suggests that this apparent contradiction can be resolved and, if properly rethought, PI can retain an important place in the metapsychology of AFT. If we consider the dialectic of recognition to be at the centre of therapeutic action, PI is the concept in psychoanalysis that best describes it. In order to highlight this theoretical and heuristic value of PI, the proposal is to re-read it in the light of some of Merleau-Ponty's concepts (chiasmus, entanglement, intercorporeality, flesh of the world, etc.) that emphasize the essentially social and embodied texture of subjectivity.

responseSet.title: The Interface Function
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interface Function

Uta Karacaoğlan and Erwin-Josef Speckmann

Erwin-Josef Speckmann

Gutenbergstr. 55, 50823 Köln, Germany
karacaoglan@t-online.de

Münster, Germany
karacaoglan@t-online.de

Erwin-Josef Speckmann  

Interface function, flow between two spaces, entropy, difference, contact-barrier, α-function

Interfaces connect spaces that must both be sufficiently similar and different. The differences are the driving force for an exchange between the spaces. In doing so, interfaces control the quantity and quality of the exchange that takes place through them. In terms of spatial and temporal dimensions, interfaces are highly complex, self-organising, and finitely extended. The interface-function is a highly specialised process that enables difference-based exchange. At the driving end, it requires energy from outside, which reduces entropy and increases information, and in this way creates and maintains difference. The interface-function as a basic principle is being employed here metaphorically to develop the hypothesis that there is an analogous basic function in the psyche since interfaces are ubiquitous in the entire organism, i.e. brain and body, and function simultaneously. Transferring the assumption of the interface-function to mental processes postulates difference as a basic principle. We will illustrate our reflections here with the help of clinical vignettes from psychoanalytic treatments thus illuminating the interface-function’s various properties and some aspects of its disruption and restoration.

responseSet.title: “What Do his Lips Want from Me?” Infantile Sexuality and Enigmatic Messages in Psychoanalytic Parent–Infant Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“What Do his Lips Want from Me?” Infantile Sexuality and Enigmatic Messages in Psychoanalytic Parent–Infant Psychotherapy

Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

Stockholm, Sweden
bjorn.salomonsson@ki.se
bjornsalomonsson50@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4747-541X

Björn Salomonsson  

Infantile sexuality, enigmatic messages, psychoanalytic parent-infant psychotherapy

This paper’s clinical material derives from psychoanalytic parent–infant psychotherapy (PIP). It applies Freud’s concept of infantile sexuality to clinical processes in PIP. Freud’s sources were everyday baby observations and adult patients’ childhood accounts. He was unclear as to when infantile sexuality emerged in babies, claiming it was observable in the newborn as well as unobservable only until about three years of age. Furthermore, he argued that the sexual drive leant on the survival instinct, thereby adding erotic pleasure to breastfeeding. This process remained unclear until Laplanche suggested a traffic of enigmatic messages in dyadic interactions. Their meanings were unconscious to the adult and incomprehensible to the baby. The paper investigates if the baby might experience certain communicative expressions as especially enigmatic. It applies an observational and interpretative method, layered analysis, to a clip of a video-recorded PIP session. It shows the analyst approaching a gaze-avoidant baby by greeting and smiling. These efforts were conscious to him. In contrast, as his lips made two kiss-like motions they were unconscious to him and retroactively interpreted as signifying his infantile sexuality. The paper discusses if such micro-events correspond to Laplanche’s enigmatic messages.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis, Criminology and Delinquency: The Early History of the Portman Clinic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Criminology and Delinquency: The Early History of the Portman Clinic

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London, UK
jyakeley@aol.com

Jessica Yakeley  

Portman Clinic, delinquency, criminology, Edward Glover, Grace Pailthorpe, Melitta Schmideberg

Founded in 1933, the Portman Clinic, now part of the Tavistock and Portman NHS Foundation Trust, in London UK, is a nationally funded out-patient clinic providing psychoanalytic psychotherapy to children, adolescents and adults who present with delinquency, violence, and problematic sexual behaviours. The Portman Clinic came into being during the interwar years, a fertile time in which psychoanalytic theories became influential within criminology. This article describes the foundation and early history of the Clinic within the wider social and political context of the early and mid-twentieth century, including the impact of the second world war and the dawn of the welfare state. It explores the ideas of the psychoanalysts Grace Pailthorpe, Edward Glover, Kate Friedlander, and Melitta Schmideberg, which were based on their work with patients at the Portman Clinic but were also shaped by the internal war within the British psychoanalytic community, the so-called Controversial Discussions. The review draws on previously unpublished clinical material from archived records of patients seen at the Portman Clinic since 1933, providing a fascinating glimpse into the profile of these patients, and how their psychopathology and offending behaviours were influenced by changing societal norms and significant historical events.

Bernard Reith  

Setting, remote psychoanalysis, teleanalysis, digital, ethics, research

The introduction of remote communication technology in psychoanalytic practice and training raises multiple psychoanalytic, clinical, and ethical issues that require qualitative and empirical process and outcome research, careful psychoanalytic reflection, and open but transparent searching dialogue between practitioners.

responseSet.title: The Phenomenology of Teleanalysis: A Research Study of the Experiences of Analysts and Candidates in Training Analyses in the US During 2 Years of the COVID-19 Pandemic
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Phenomenology of Teleanalysis: A Research Study of the Experiences of Analysts and Candidates in Training Analyses in the US During 2 Years of the COVID-19 Pandemic

Nicolle Zapien

Nicolle Zapien

San Francisco, CA, USA
drzapien@nicollegottfriedzapien.com
nicolle.zapien@gmail.com
5625 College Ave. Suite 216F, Oakland, CA 94618

Nicolle Zapien  

Teleanalysis, COVID-19, Giorgi, phenomenology, training, frame

There has historically been controversy among psychoanalysts about the use and efficacy of teleanalysis. As COVID-19 arrived, most analysts offered teleanalysis despite their opinions and any prior experiences of this format of analytic care. Twelve psychoanalysts and thirteen candidates in training analyses in the US using teleanalysis two years into the pandemic were interviewed. Giorgi’s phenomenological research method (Giorgi, A. 2009. The Descriptive Phenomenological Method for Psychology. Pittsburgh, PA: Duquesne Press) was used to develop a generalizable description of teleanalysis including its constituent parts. Results suggest that teleanalysis allows more access to treatments at analytic frequency, but it is not equivalent to in-person analysis as there are aspects that are felt to be missing and important. The impact of the medium, including increased distractions, difficulties with maintaining the frame, and missing aspects, are felt by both analysts and candidates in training analyses, but are even more starkly felt by candidate analysands than analysts. Providing teleanalysis presents unique challenges to the analyst due to less control of the teleanalytic frame and the need to represent and analyze in some way what is missing. Analyzing the unconscious motivations, dynamics, and meanings of the use of teleanalysis (or in-person or hybrid analysis) may deepen the treatment regardless of what format is ultimately used.

[[RunningHead]]

What we Don’t Talk about Enough when we Talk about Teleanalysis: A Response to “The Phenomenology of Teleanalysis” by Dr N. Zapien

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Queen Anne Street Practice, 55 Queen Anne Street, London W1
alemma@mac.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Teleanalysis, ethics, body

I am grateful to the Editors for inviting me to respond to Dr Zapien’s illuminating research investigating analysts’ and candidates’ experience of “teleanalysis” during the COVID pandemic. This research makes an important contribution to the growing analytic literature on teleanalysis, which is the umbrella term used by Dr Zapien to capture the use of “purely teleanalytic treatments, hybrid treatments, and shuttle treatments”. Dr Zapien’s research not only provides interesting insights into the experience of working “in the tents” during the pandemic, to use Bolognini’s (2020) apt turn of phrase, but also affords us an opportunity to reflect on the gains and losses of continuing to practise teleanalysis now that the pandemic is no longer the force majeure driving the shift to a teleanalytic setting.

Faithful to the phenomenological method Dr Zapien opts for in this research, she offers us an unsaturated account of the experience of her participants without imposing a theoretical or evaluative frame. In responding to her findings, I am inevitably interpreting them as I selectively focus, first, on the question of whether it is possible to engage an analytic process in teleanalysis and, second, on the ethical risks of teleanalysis.

The fate of the analytic process in teleanalysis1

In the early days of Skype and telephone sessions, there was considerable concern about how these media compromised the process such that what was being offered was “not really psychoanalysis” (Lemma and Caparrotta 2014). Some analysts underlined the fundamental differences between the in-person and remote modes of communication. For example, in her nuanced exploration of technology-mediated communication, Brahnam concluded: “In a way, in-person communication and distant communication resemble each other as much as whole foods resemble highly processed foods” (2017, 147), underscoring how technological mediation leads to a “fracture(ing) of the analytic field” (Brahnam 2017: 148). responseSet.title: Revisiting the Constancy of Analytic Setting in a Changing World
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revisiting the Constancy of Analytic Setting in a Changing World

Clara Nemas

Clara Nemas

Dr Tomás Manuel de Anchorena 1463, 9 B, Buenos Aires 1425, República Argentina
claranemas@gmail.com

Clara Nemas  

Frame, online sessions, internal setting

This series, proposed by the Educational Committee, explores different perspectives on the concept of setting, addressing the transformations it underwent following the COVID-19 pandemic. During the pandemic, most psychoanalysts faced the unprecedented challenge of not only altering the traditional way of conducting sessions but also adapting to new technologies, often unfamiliar to them. However, the evolution of the setting is not solely a response to crisis. Technological advancements have enabled psychoanalysis to reach individuals in regions where it was previously unavailable. Additionally, patients who emigrate often seek continuity in their analysis, which resulted in the need to maintain sessions remotely. Likewise, individuals living abroad have the chance to engage in analysis in their native language, leading to what has been termed long-distance analysis. This article presents four papers by psychoanalysts from diverse backgrounds, offering insights into the impact on psychoanalytic practice.

responseSet.title: Vicissitudes of the Psychoanalytic Setting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vicissitudes of the Psychoanalytic Setting11

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez

Barcelona, Spain
aps.nijar@gmail.com

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez  

Psychoanalytic setting, face-to-face setting, distance setting, psychoanalytic method

The outbreak of the pandemic forced a temporary change from an in-person setting to a distance setting via a screen, generating a debate about the viability of the psychoanalytic process in the case of distance settings. This discussion leads us to reconsider the fundamental elements of the setting, such as the hitherto unquestioned co-presence of the bodies of the patient and the analyst in the same space. After reviewing key moments in the evolution of the concept, from Freud to Bleger, and analysing the contemporary situation, including recent IPA reports, it is emphasised that the physical presence of the body, in which psychic life is rooted, is essential to enable unconscious communication. To support this idea, clinical examples and a detailed vignette are presented. In addition, the importance of the frequency of sessions is highlighted, underlining the fact that the quantitative factor is crucial to the nature of psychic life and any attempt to modify it.

responseSet.title: Frame as a Commonly Shared Transitional Object: Reflections on Remote Analysis/Teleanalysis and the Corona Crisis Using a Case Example
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Frame as a Commonly Shared Transitional Object: Reflections on Remote Analysis/Teleanalysis and the Corona Crisis Using a Case Example

Bernd Pütz

Bernd Pütz

Berlin, Germany
berndpuetz@web.de

Bernd Pütz  

Frame, transitional object, teleanalysis

This paper attempts to use Winnicott's theory of the transitional object to develop a concept of the psychoanalytic frame as a transitional object commonly shared by analyst and analysand. The process of creating the frame, often neglected in theories, is characterized by different phases. After the interview, a phase is postulated in which the symmetrical aspects of the analytic relationship are considered crucial for the formation of a frame: the commonly shared transitional object. In a following phase, the asymmetry of the relationship is considered significant: the analyst takes over the protection of the frame. Using the example of a patient who called by telephone from a moving car during his session, reflections will be made about teleanalysis/remote analysis and the consequences of the Covid-19 pandemic for psychoanalytic treatment techniques. Finally, the need for considerations about a psychoanalytic media theory with reflections on the medium “telephone” is emphasized.

responseSet.title: When Remote Analysis is Used as an Attack on the Frame
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When Remote Analysis is Used as an Attack on the Frame

Kristin White

Kristin White

Holsteiner Ufer 34, 10557 Berlin, Germany
Kristin.White@t-online.de

Kristin White  

Claustro-agoraphobia, remote psychotherapy, frame

Henri Rey’s theory of claustro-agoraphobia is helpful for the understanding of the difficulties that can arise when the frame of treatment is modified in remote analysis or psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The felt intense closeness and the actual distance of remote analysis lend themselves to a defensive position in remote analysis when the patient feels at once trapped in the analysis and yet unable to be without the analysis. I suggest here that such claustro-agoraphobic fears arise particularly at times in the analytic process in which change is imminent. The patient is then confronted with giving up old systems of defence. Moving away and changing to remote analysis can provide a relief in the face of the simultaneous fear of closeness and of distance. With such patients, the two steps of moving away and changing to remote analysis can be seen as an unconscious attack on the frame by a part of the patient that is defending itself against such fears in the face of change. Once the setting has changed in remote psychotherapy, the analyst too can be drawn into the claustro-agoraphobic position in the face of the patient’s attacks on the analyst’s symbolic thinking and his internal frame.

responseSet.title: The Frame in Analysis: On Lacan’s Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Frame in Analysis: On Lacan’s Perspective

Paola Mieli

Paola Mieli

New York, USA
parolapm@yahoo.com

Paola Mieli  

Subject supposed to know, time, scansion, preliminary sessions, drives, object

The fundamental rule requiring analysands to say whatever passes through their mind has a crucial counterpart on the side of the analyst in the “evenly suspended attention”, where having an ear finely tuned to unconscious processes, as Freud describes it, implies a suspension of any acquired knowledge. Such an attention is orientated to the sequence of the analysand’s uttered signifiers, in a listening attuned to what produces effects of sense and non-sense beyond the intention of the discourse. The possibility of sustaining the position of not-knowing is, according to Lacan, what constitutes the very frame of the analytic process. The analyst’s rigorous listening brings to the fore its own logic and the temporality associated with it.

responseSet.title: Some Reflections on the Nature of Psychoanalytic Debate in Academic Publishing: A Response to Roberto D’Angelo’s: “Do we Want to Know?”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Reflections on the Nature of Psychoanalytic Debate in Academic Publishing: A Response to Roberto D’Angelo’s: “Do we Want to Know?”

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
alemma@mac.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Dear Editor,

I write in relation to the recent publication of the paper by Roberto D’Angelo (2024): “Do we want to know?”. In this paper, he invites us collectively to consider the destructive currents in psychoanalytic debates about trans-identified young people. He highlights one outcome of these debates, namely that they “ … obscure or deny the psychic pain that is sometimes humming beneath the experience of gender dysphoria” (1). He further draws attention to, and illustrates, how some clinicians “ … misrepresent and attempt to discredit clinicians who explore the meaning and function of trans identification, or who express concern that transitioning may be a drastic solution to various forms of psychic pain” (1).

This paper is timely and important not only because it encourages us to critically consider how we approach the question of transgender within our discipline, but also because it provides us with a powerful illustration of a more insidious problem that arises in other contemporary debates that divide our discipline. First, I will address the specific question D’Angelo raises with respect to debates about transgender. I will then share some general reflections about how we debate. I conclude with some thoughts about the importance of protecting the integrity of academic publishing to support reasoned debate.

As a recipient of the kind of “discrediting” that D’Angelo refers to, I am familiar with the dynamic he highlights, and which has created an unhelpful climate for thinking about how to best support transgender individuals. In one important sense, the experience of the clinician charged as “transphobic” is the least of the problems we face. It’s not a comfortable position when one is on the receiving end of this, but it comes with the territory. The graver problem, as D’Angelo underlines, is that categorical positions which do not allow for the range of experiences we encounter clinically, neglect the pain reported by those transgender individuals for whom transitioning did not provide a more hospitable home for the self. We need to ask ourselves why we silence these voices such that some transgender individuals are also anxious about sharing their experiences of doubt or regret. The moving account by Jules Shaper (2024) of his experience of transitioning is a very powerful example of one such voice. Even if, for the sake of argument, only a minority of transgender individuals feel that transitioning was not the solution to their pain, should this not, at the very least, compel us to be more nuanced in how we think rather than take up reductive, polarised positions?

responseSet.title: Lost and Found in Translation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lost and Found in Translation

Gregorio Kohon

Gregorio Kohon

guershn7@gmail.com

Gregorio Kohon  

Similarly to other psychoanalytic authors, Kets de Vries referred to “Freud’s dictum that the ‘normal’ personality should possess the ability to love and work … ” (2016, 70).

A dictum: a short statement that expresses a general truth or principle. Nevertheless, this particular “dictum” seems not to have ever been created by Freud — in fact, it may have never existed as such. And yet, throughout the years, it has become a fairly popular psychoanalytic slogan.

In 1950, Erik H. Erikson wrote:

Freud was once asked what he thought a normal person should be able to do well. The questioner probably expected a complicated, “deep” answer. But Freud simply said, “Lieben und Arbeiten” (“to love and to work”). It pays to ponder on this simple formula; it grows deeper as you think about it. For when Freud said “love,” he meant the generosity of intimacy as well as genital love … ” (Erikson, 1950 (1993), 229).

It is not known whether that particular imaginary scene ever took place. However, many psychoanalytic authors, academic teachers and others, while admitting that they could not find a bibliographic reference to this “quotation” from Freud, have enthusiastically referred to this “simple formula” in their writings. Erikson’s suggestion became a source of “wisdom”. At times, it was quoted in the popular press.

In the late 70s, I asked Jill Duncan, a former Librarian at the London Institute of Psychoanalysis, to help me to search for this quotation. Recently, I found amongst my papers a note from her, which I had forgotten having. After a laborious search, Jill discovered a paper by Leo Rangell, who wrote:

Asked once to name the most essential ingredients for mental health, Freud responded with his famous brief communication, “ … the capacity for achievement and enjoyment” (Leistung und Genuss). Through a series of loose translations this has been watered down and is now often referred to as “to work and to love” … (a) popularised and corrupt version … ” (Rangell, 1963, JAPA, p. 50).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Understanding of Trauma: Post-Traumatic Mental Functioning, the Zero Process, and the Construction of Reality, by Joseph Fernando, 2023, Routledge, London and New York, 231 pp., $39.95 (paperback), ISBN: 978-1-032-25442-5

Thomas Helscher

Thomas Helscher

drhelscher@gmail.com

Thomas Helscher  

Freudian drive theory has found an important voice in the work of Joseph Fernando on what he calls the “zero process drive” and the “zero process defenses”. This book offers a detailed clinical and metapsychological description of how the mind responds to trauma, integrating Freudian and ego psychological drive and defence theory with some current thought about how the brain processes memory. In bridging neuroscience, contemporary trauma treatments such as eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) and sensorimotor processing, and classical drive theory, Fernando offers an exciting new psychoanalytic way of thinking about and treating trauma that serves as an important adjunct to other approaches. Fernando coins a new term, “the zero process drive”, to argue that traumatic functioning constitutes a mental process equivalent to Freud’s primary and secondary processes. This is an important contribution to psychoanalytic thinking, one that shifts our attention away from developmental or relational processes, and redirects us to the more contingent, particular, and event-focused dynamics of trauma. Although Fernando’s relentless focus on drive elements unnecessarily minimizes the role of the other in both the trauma and its recovery, his emphasis on the functioning of the zero process drive opens important avenues for the psychoanalytic treatment of traumatic experiences.

Fernando begins his analysis with a careful description of what trauma is and, more important, what it is not, in order to clear the field for a more precise description of how trauma operates in the mind. He differentiates his approach to trauma from Bion’s theory of beta elements and alpha function, arguing that Bion and those who draw on his work conflate the normal developmental process of the creation of what Bion calls “an apparatus for thinking thoughts” with the uniquely pathological process the mind undergoes in trauma. Fernando begins his theoretical description of “The Traumatic Process” with a detailed case example that he follows throughout the book. He then locates the historical emergence of the study of trauma in the work of Pierre Janet, who described how

key characteristics of trauma were a product of the failure to assimilate an incident that was too far outside the experiences of the person, and that because of this, actions and feelings that would have been appropriate to the situation were not performed or felt. Symptoms involved versions of the truncated actions and feelings appearing in the present. (11)

Steven Groarke

sgroarke2@gmail.com

Steven Groarke  

We can speak sensibly enough of Kleinians and Lacanians, and need not bat an eyelid in describing someone as a Freudian. But I am not at all sure what being a Winnicottian means. I found myself mulling this question over while reading Christopher Bollas’s 1980s Rome lectures, recently published as Essential Aloneness (2024). Is Bollas now or has he ever been a Winnicottian? The more insidious implications of my question are evident in its McCarthyite tenor. I should like to loosen the question from its accusatory or persecutory moorings, however, and use it instead as a way of approaching the lectures in a spirit of free inquiry. As Daniel Widlöcher has pointed out, no other psychoanalyst has considered more deeply than Winnicott the path that remains open in subjective experience via the 454

[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Lisbon

Conceição de Almeida

Conceição de Almeida

Lisbon, Portugal
conceicaotavaresdealmeida@gmail.com

Conceição de Almeida  

Identity, navigate, saudade, colonialism, freedom, crisis

Using the title of the 54th International Psychoanalytical Association Congress as a motif, the author associates and develops metaphors that illustrate and relate how the historical elements of Portuguese culture may have a psychoanalytic understanding and how they impacted the Portuguese Psychoanalytic Society’s identity and work. Past and future, pride and sorrow, land and sea seem to shape the Portuguese psyche, creating a welcoming and conciliating nature forged from the tensions underlying troubled waters longing to be sailed.

responseSet.title: Climate Anxieties and their Countertransference Challenges in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Climate Anxieties and their Countertransference Challenges in Psychoanalysis

Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

Tomtebodavägen 18A, 171 77 Stockholm, Sweden
bjorn.salomonsson@ki.se
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4747-541X

Gaveliusgatan 11, 116 41 Stockholm, Sweden

Björn Salomonsson  

Climate change, countertransference, solastalgia, anxiety, depressive position, Searles

The paper discusses countertransference issues when patients’ anxieties about global warming appear in the psychoanalytic situation. Both analyst and analysand are affected, concretely and emotionally, by manifestations of climate change. It is argued that when a patient’s climate anxieties surface in treatment, the analyst may end up in a peculiar countertransference. The analyst, whose task is to discern and address any anxieties in the patient, may simultaneously note climate anxieties in himself or herself. There may thus be two anxious people in the consulting room, and both may use unconscious defences against awareness of climate destruction. This may explain the observation that concerns about climate change seem rarely expressed – implicitly or explicitly – in the analytic situation. Alternatively, patients do express them, but the analyst does not discern them. The paper discusses the psychoanalyst’s technique and stance when analysing climate anxieties in patients. This presupposes that the clinician analyses apathy and solastalgia in connection with his/her own anxieties, as well as his/her oscillating between hope and hopelessness about climate restitution. His/her capacity of enduring the pain of the depressive position will be put to the test.

responseSet.title: Hearing Oneself Speak the Never-Before Spoken: Therapeutic Action from a Lacanian Perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hearing Oneself Speak the Never-Before Spoken: Therapeutic Action from a Lacanian Perspective

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

Los Angeles, CA, USA
richardtuch@gmail.com

Los Angeles, CA, USA

Richard Tuch  

Lacan, therapeutic action, punctuation

Lacanian theory is dense and abstract, making it hard to discern how Lacanian analysts conduct treatment. This paper outlines basic Lacanian methods, doing so using the framework of therapeutic action—how analysis affects psychic changes as seen from a Lacanian perspective. Lacan was opposed to the clinical goal of heightening a patient's self-understanding. His clinical efforts focused more on essential knowledge (discovering “what is”) as opposed to complex knowledge (explaining why “what is” is as it is). Two mutative clinical events are discussed: speaking the unspoken and movement from empty to full speech. Core Lacanian methods of bringing about such events include: “punctuation” (echoing noteworthy patient statements that contain more than the patient imagines), the use of oracular speech (“curve balls” that challenge a patient's core assumptions), and unplanned clinical moments when the analyst fails to live up to the patient's expectations (to be the one who is supposed to know). Basic Lacanian theory is outlined to gain a sense of what Lacanians see as the core human ailment: a sense of alienation and the endless search to satisfy desire that stems from one's core sense of lack—the sense of being perennially incomplete.

responseSet.title: Musical Creativity in the Line of Fire
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Musical Creativity in the Line of Fire

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

London, UK
fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

Musically aesthetic containment, musical creativity, L, H, K and O

Music and war is a vast topic. This essay limits itself mainly to compositions of Western classical music written in response to World War II. I examine two masterpieces: A Ceremony of Carols (1942), by UK pacifist, Benjamin Britten, composed on a ship menaced by U-boats as he returned from voluntary exile to face the war; and the Quartet for the End of Time (1941), by the Frenchman, Olivier Messiaen, interned in a German prisoner-of-war camp, where 400 prisoners and German officers attended its premiere in freezing conditions. I refer also to Bion’s wartime experiences.

War is studied as a magnification of hatreds and rivalry within families, focusing on the biblical lament of David for the killing of his son Absalom, set to music in masterpieces down the centuries. Finally, Britten’s War Requiem is explored, as a modern testament to the horrors of war, avoiding pomp and emphasising ambivalence and ambiguity.

I consider these pieces from the perspective of Bion’s theories of L, H, K and O, and suggest they bear out not only Winnicott’s theory of transitional space but particularly Segal’s theory of artistic creativity emanating from the depressive position with its depiction of a ruined world, which the music may attempt to begin to repair, particularly through the operation of musically aesthetic listening and containment, a notion which is developed in the paper.

responseSet.title: A session with Lucia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A session with Lucia

Stefano Bolognini

Stefano Bolognini

Bologna, Italy
dott.stefano.bolognini@gmail.com

Stefano Bolognini  

Re-analysis, oedipal and pre-oedipal mixed complexity, collateral transference

The clinical material concerns a session with a patient undergoing re-analysis six years after the end of the previous treatment period: analyst and patient are the same persons, but both find themselves profoundly changed after the first experience. For the patient, what is at stake is the deepening and greater articulation of internal contact with self and other, together with the recovery of a greater sense of continuity and cohesion in the sense of self. For the analyst, this re-analysis is also an opportunity to confront one's own evolution in functioning with both the working Ego and the working Self. The reported session touches on various experiential and technical levels, with references to the extra- and intra-analytic past, to extra- and intra-analytic events in the here and now, and with the constitution of a growing subjectivity that hints at the prospect of a much more substantial separation than that of the first analysis.

responseSet.title: Transformative Encounters: Bridging Cyber- and Analytic Space
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transformative Encounters: Bridging Cyber- and Analytic Space**

Sun Ju Chung

Sun Ju Chung

Seoul, South Korea
sunju629@gmail.com

Sun Ju Chung  

Technology and psychoanalysis, Transitional space, Countertransference, Technique

This paper explores the transformative impact of recognizing and bridging patients’ cyber-lives to the analytic space, especially in the context of analysis with children and adolescents. With the widespread use of digital technology in the lives of young patients, analysts are urged to gain a deeper understanding of the role of cyberspace in their psychic lives and to consider incorporating digital devices as a mode of communication within the analytic setting. Through two detailed case materials, the author illustrates how integrating patients’ cyberspace into the analytic space plays a crucial role in their analytic journey to facilitate constructing potential space and symbolic functioning in their minds. The paper also demonstrates various countertransference challenges that analysts may encounter when technology or its influence is present in the analytic space.

responseSet.title: Panel Report from the 54th IPA Congress in Lisbon 2025: “Finding One’s Voice in Chaotic Times”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report from the 54th IPA Congress in Lisbon 2025: “Finding One’s Voice in Chaotic Times”

Kristin White

Kristin White

Berlin, Germany
Kristin.White@t-online.de

Kristin White  

Chair: Donald Moss

Discussant: M. Fakhry Davids

Presenters: Anton Hart, M. Fakhry Davids

Reporter: Kristin White

The panel “Finding one’s voice in chaotic times” was led by three expert writers and researchers on the topics of racism, racial issues, diversity and otherness: Donald Moss and Anton Hart from New York and M. Fakhry Davids from London. They describe themselves in their introduction as “three senior analysts, one born as a Black South African Muslim, one born as an African-American Jew and one born as a white American Jew”. We were told at the beginning of the panel discussion that the three panellists had worked together for some years, discussing their clinical work and their papers together. The warmth and openness of the conversations between the discussants was clearly a product of their longstanding working relationship. In the panel discussion itself, the discussants certainly found their voices with an honest and critical discussion of their own and each other’s clinical material.

However, the panel discussion began with a practical exercise in dealing with what it felt like being othered and unwanted and not having a place to stay in chaotic times: The room that had been allocated to this panel discussion was far too small to hold the around 90 participants. Those who came first took one of the 30 or so chairs. The others tried to bring in chairs from the hallway. But this posed a fire risk according to the wardens, so we then all tried to move out into a larger space in the hallway, taking just a few chairs for those who needed them most. The rest of us sat on the floor. But very soon we were ousted from this space, too, it being apparently a work space for the organisers. It felt a bit like an experience of being migrants with no fixed abode being told repeatedly to move on. Back in the original room, with most of us squeezed together on the limited floor space (again a reminder of the cramped living spaces allocated to the unwanted peoples of our world), the panel discussion was able to begin, albeit around 20 min late. The offer from the panellists that they could make up some of the time at the end was not really a solution for many of the participants who had meetings and seminars to attend directly after the ending of this panel discussion. Somebody in the team of organisers must have grossly misjudged the amount of interest in this particular panel discussion.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Report on Panel “Serious Trauma and Life-Threatening Illness in the Analyst: Reclaiming Anchorage in Life and Work”, Lisbon, 2025**

Carolina Bacchi and Amrita Narayanan

Amrita Narayanan

San Francisco, CA, USA
drbacchi@gmail.com

Goa, India
dr.amritanarayanan@gmail.com

Amrita Narayanan  

Analyst's illness, psychotherapy, termination

In setting the stage for this panel, Harvey Schwartz used an expression that, as analysts, we sometimes repress: protective self-involvement. In other words, at times, simply by virtue of being a human with their own life, the analyst needs to be self-absorbed. What happens then in an analysis when the reality of the analyst’s physical body (illness, accidents or self-preoccupations) impinges on the treatment, requiring analysts to divert attention and care from the patients to themselves? The speakers on this panel reflected on the vulnerability of the physical body; the pressure analysts are under to deny the reality of the time-limited human condition out of respect to the timelessness of the analytic process; the transformative clinical moments; and the disruptions to the analytic dyad that the analyst’s unpredictable and unavoidable life experiences impose.

Jennifer Davids’s paper, focusing on the period of uncertainty during her evolving debilitating symptoms and absence from work, and return to clinical work, after a serious injury in a car accident, was wittily entitled “Getting Back on the Road”. She emphasised the active psychic work that the analyst needs to do in order to navigate such absence. Davids emphasises the importance of the relational network around the analyst to facilitate the maintenance of the analytic dyad. In the British Psychoanalytical Society, it is mandatory to appoint two clinical trustees who help the clinician to think about and manage such clinical situations. Davids communicated directly with her patients throughout her absence for several months and did not lose any of her patients.

Chronicling the experiences and anxieties with which she as an analyst was beset in the particular situation of recovery from injury in a car accident, Davids draws attention to the bi-directional nature of the analytic dyad and to what might be considered a role reversal: what happens when the vulnerable analyst is suddenly in the role of being a patient and needs her patients to survive the reality of her absence due to illness? During such a traumatic experience, the sense of time is often altered, for example experienced as frozen and endless, for both analysand and analyst. Davids discussed the technical dilemmas of re-union and of encouraging some patients to express their fantasies when they might be afraid to upset their analyst.

responseSet.title: The International Journal: A Psychoanalytic Anchor in Chaotic Times
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The International Journal: A Psychoanalytic Anchor in Chaotic Times

Catherine Humble

Catherine Humble

Catherine.Humble@iopa.org.uk

Catherine Humble  

publishing, social, splitting, omnipotence, ideology, Roger Money-Kyrle

Psychoanalysis is currently at a turbulent moment, with challenges from the ‘external’ world, and from within psychoanalysis, as it becomes increasingly pluralist and contradictory, said Francis Grier, Editor-in Chief of the IJP.

The aim of the IJP panel was to explore the function of the journal as an anchor for the international psychoanalytic community, exploring its role in containing different – and even antagonistic – strands of psychoanalytic thought.

Heinz Weiss, Editor of the IJP Education Section, opened the presentations.

Recent years have seen unprecedented social crises, creating a paradigm shift and disrupting our certitude, said Weiss. We seem plunged into an “uncertainty cloud,” as Bion called it. Can psychoanalysis help us find our way through?

In light of these crises, the IJP Education section has taken up certain pertinent topics, inviting commentaries with divergent views. By doing so, Weiss said, we hope to offer a sort of containment and ‘anchor’, and space for emerging ideas.

One topic explored was the concept of the supergo. Examining the superego as a hinge between the individual and =society, the IJP considered its pathological organisations, and how its psychotic and perverse elements might help us to understand the recent impact of digital media, virtual reality and propaganda.

In 1934, after attending propaganda speeches by Hitler and Goebbels, Roger Money-Kyrle wrote “The Psychological Causes of War.” The work is stunningly topical, said Weiss, addressing the role of splitting, projection and manic defences against paranoid fears. The IJP Education section published Money Kyrle’s piece, along with a collection of papers on Freud’s “Why war?”, plus a paper on human destructiveness, configuring war as a breakdown in symbolic thinking and return to primitive, concrete, omnipotent mechanisms of thought.

In recent years, with Covid and new digital technologies, the analytic setting has come under threat. In response, the Education section published papers re-examining the frame, considering both how it is endangered and how it might be preserved.

responseSet.title: Tales of the Triangle. Thoughts on Essentials of Psychoanalysis Across Cultures
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tales of the Triangle. Thoughts on Essentials of Psychoanalysis Across Cultures

Tomas Plaenkers

Tomas Plaenkers

Frankfurt, Germany
info@praxis-dr-plaenkers.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9665-8514

Tomas Plaenkers  

Oedipus complex, myths, cultural identity

The paper explores the universal validity of the Oedipus complex in different cultures, particularly in Asia, and discusses the role of psychoanalysis as an anchor in chaotic times. The author delves into the objections raised by Asian cultures to the universal validity of the Oedipus complex. They provide examples from Indian, Chinese, and Japanese myths that challenge Freud's concept. The author argues that these myths and cultural variations are not antipodes to the Oedipus complex but rather cultural variants of a universal triangularity. The Eastern myths suggest that the early mother-child relationship plays a central role in cultural development and assigns women a significant role, contrary to Freud's views. Furthermore, the author challenges the notion of cultural identity between the West and the East as incommensurable. The paper concludes by discussing the need for psychoanalysis to address the individual and social turns against triangularity.

responseSet.title: Experiencing Recognition and Suffering Non-Recognition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Experiencing Recognition and Suffering Non-Recognition**

Louise Gyler

Louise Gyler

Sydney, Australia
louise.gyler@bigpond.com

Louise Gyler  

Recognition, identity, trauma

This paper explores the significance of recognition through associated questions about identity, identifications and what is needed to give an account of oneself from a psychoanalytical and philosophical perspective. These questions are explored through clinical work with a small child and the artistic practice of Waanyi artist Judy Watson arguing that both share the desire to work creatively with residues of fragmentary experiences to enable remembering and recognition. Their work illustrates the suffering inflicted by non-recognition as well as the desire and creative efforts required to establish a precondition for recognition when this desire has been elided and/or silenced. Possible implications for Australian socio-cultural life are introduced.

responseSet.title: Beneath the Harmonious Surface: Exploring Korea’s Deep-Rooted Strains
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beneath the Harmonious Surface: Exploring Korea’s Deep-Rooted Strains

In-Soo Lee

In-Soo Lee

Seoul, South Korea
psychoanalysis.lee@gmail.com

In-Soo Lee  

Korean culture, confucianism, narcissistic vulnerability

Korean culture, influenced significantly by Confucianism, has shaped the psyche of Koreans in several ways. The absence of an emotionally connected father figure has led to father hunger, while the strong emotional bond between mothers and sons has contributed to fear of women and repressed Oedipal conflicts. The suppression of female sexuality, along with discriminatory attitudes towards women, has given rise to emotional conditions like “Han” and penis envy, as well as hysterical disorders. Confucianism's emphasis on rigid ideal roles and hierarchy led to the internalization of a strict superego, prioritizing external evaluations over inner experiences. The use of compartmentalization helped cope with external demands but hindered the development of an integrated sense of self. Additionally, Confucian family culture hindered the natural process of individuation, with an abrupt transition to adulthood around the age of seven, leading to a longing for lost early dependent relationships. Narcissistic vulnerability is deeply rooted in Confucian hierarchical culture, necessitating narcissistic attachment and defence mechanisms to maintain fantasies of omnipotence and self-sufficiency. Given these cultural influences and individual vulnerabilities, it is crucial to foster genuine dependency in psychoanalytic efforts while being cautious about falling into pseudo-intimacy that remains confined to intellectual interactions within a therapeutic relationships.

[[RunningHead]]

Living and Containing Psychoanalysis in Institutions: Psychoanalysts Working Together, edited by Gabrielle Junkers, 2023, London and New York, Routledge/Taylor & Francis Group Publisher, 186 pp., US $31.96 (pbk), ISBN: 9781032295138

James W Barron

James W Barron

New York, NY, USA
jwbarron101@gmail.com

New York, NY, USA

Newton Centre, MA, USA

James W Barron  

Psychoanalytic Organizations and Their Discontents

Sometimes trauma creates conditions for heightened curiosity and sustained self-inquiry. Gabriele Junkers, a psychologist and training analyst of the German Psychoanalytic Association, the editor of and contributing author to this volume, responded to the enforced restriction of in-person contact with her professional colleagues, imposed by the COVID-19 pandemic, by reflecting on the paradox of painfully missing the opportunities to meet with colleagues in various institutional contexts while simultaneously being aware of the profound discontents experienced by many members living their professional lives in and with those institutions, a unique instantiation of Bion’s trenchant observation that the individual is a group animal at war, both with the group and with those aspects of his personality that constitute his “groupishness”.

This volume resonated strongly with me because of my experience in leadership roles in various psychoanalytic organizations and my longstanding interest in applying psychoanalytic perspectives to understand organizational dynamics and leadership challenges. I find Bionian and post-Bionian field theory to be particularly helpful in locating myself and other(s) within complex fields of intersubjective processes. I also appreciate the conceptual power of systems psychodynamics, a hybrid of psychoanalytic perspectives and open systems theory, growing out of Bion’s pioneering work on groups and its elaboration through the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations over the past 75 years.

As psychoanalysts we are familiar with the intersubjective field of the clinical dyad (group of two). We are becoming increasingly aware of the porous boundaries between our internal and external worlds, and the mutual influencing and shaping of our psyches (at conscious and unconscious levels) and our social and cultural surround. Extending these ideas beyond the dyad to working within larger constellations in groups and organizations will have, I believe, significant impact on the theory and praxis of clinical psychoanalysis, and on our understanding of leadership and organizational dynamics, including our psychoanalytic organizations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Explorations of What Women Want Today. Femininity, Desire and Agency, edited by Margarita Cereijido, Paula L. Ellman and Nancy R. Goodman, New York, Routledge, 2022, 201 pp., $42.95 (paperback), ISBN 9781032017815

Dale Gody

Dale Gody

4505 Wasatch, Suite 330E, Salt Lake City 84124, UT
dalegody@gmail.com
dgody@chicagoanalysis.org

Dale Gody  

With a diverse collection of voices and a polysemy of theoretical, socio-cultural, and political perspectives, this curated collection of articles from international psychoanalysts participating in the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis of IPA (COWAP) tackles Freud’s enduring conundrum, “What do women want?” The contributors to the book make clear that this question itself is problematic from a number of perspectives. First, it frames women as the Other; second, it assumes universal wants and desires for women; and, third, it overlooks the complexity of how gender and sexuality along with culture and power differentials become imbricated in any one person’s desire and agency. The authors highlight the androcentric nature of Freud’s question, the who and when as well as the what, all of which are complicated and influenced by socio-cultural factors. The authors work to encourage analytic thinkers and practitioners to move from binaries and dualities about sexuality and gender to pluralities and complexities.

The book is divided into several sections and organized around different themes, allowing the reader to home in on what interests her, him, them; or it can be read from cover to cover as a compendium of different voices around ideas of femininity, desire, and agency. Margarita Cereijido’s introduction addresses important questions related to “what do women want today” by deconstructing the question and highlighting who is asking this question, what is a woman, and what are iterations of the feminine. She highlights the difference between “woman” and “feminine”, suggesting that each woman develops her own conception of what is feminine and that this is largely influenced by subjective social constructions. Also included in the introduction is an article by Virginia Ungar, who served as the first woman president of IPA, entitled “Authority, Power and Gender in Institutional Life”. This article traces the historical conundrum of women being silenced and denied their voices as well as excluded from spaces of power, and ends with a consideration of the role of authority in analysis and in leadership within analytic institutes.

responseSet.title: Crises and Enactments while Ending Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Crises and Enactments while Ending Treatment

Jill Salberg

Jill Salberg

155 West 71st. Street Suite 1D New York, NY 10023, USA
jillsalberg@gmail.com

Jill Salberg  

Enactment, attachment, analytic dyad, bastion

The difficulties involved in ending treatment are complex and can present a set of challenges and possibilities for both analyst and patient. When early trauma is part of the clinical picture, ending treatment may very well stir an experience of an earlier attachment rupture or loss. The author presents a case of a long-term analytic patient where an acute enactment crisis occurred causing a pause in ending the treatment. It is proposed that these very ‘ending enactments’ become necessary, crucially disrupting a bastion (Baranger & Baranger, 2009) formed by the dyad. This tearing/ending of the analytic frame (Bleger, 1967) may, catalyze a 'second look' which revealed a more chronic enactment. Ultimately more deeply buried pieces of the early relational trauma became accessible to the analytic process. The author argues that sometimes this very upheaval is needed to unblock a bastion. Thus, what was sequestered, and unknowable may enter the dyad as an enactment. In this way, insight, and the self-reflectiveness it entails, may occur post-enactment.

responseSet.title: Sunflowers Don’t Always Seek the Sun
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sunflowers Don’t Always Seek the Sun**

Maria Inês Neuenschwander Carneiro

Maria Inês Neuenschwander Carneiro

Brazil
minec620@gmail.com

Maria Inês Neuenschwander Carneiro  

Aesthetic demands, obesity, repulsion, atypical eating patterns in primitive relationships, analytic process, transference, atypical relationship with foods, “anorexia” as a symptom, cessation in emotional development

The following text addresses atypical eating, building upon previously discussed ideas and enhanced with clinical observations from the analytical process of a young man. Currently twenty years old, he was referred at sixteen by the paediatrician who had delivered him and continued to care for him, and was suspecting “anorexia.” The term is within inverted commas as it was not conclusively diagnosed by the doctor or the author of this text. The patient's limited food intake was part of a broader picture of cessation in emotional development. The author proposes significant differences in the primitive relational trajectory at the origins of atypical eating habits of both men and women. Viewing these habits as communications of previously compromised mental states rather than an isolated “disease,” the author emphasises the importance of the transferential relationship as a general guiding thread in analytical processes, particularly as a sine qua non condition for augmenting the possibility of favourable evolution of these generally severe and challenging patients. This clinical case thus highlights the analytical relationship as the main modifying factor in Fred's life. The analysis continues uninterrupted, with three sessions per week, during the pandemic online, and again in-person as soon as it was possible to return to the office.

[[RunningHead]]

The Representation of Pandemic Anxieties in a Psychoanalytic Group Psychodrama for Adolescents and Young Adults

Olivier Taieb MD PHD , Jeanne Defontaine PHD , Catherine Le Du , Mohammad Mouma and Thierry Baubet MD PHD

Thierry Baubet

Bobigny, CESP Inserm U1018, France
129 rue de Stalingrad, 93000
olivier.taieb@aphp.fr
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-0778-1079

Paris, France

Paris, France

Bobigny, CESP Inserm U1018, France

Thierry Baubet  

Psychoanalytic psychodrama, adolescence, Covid-19 pandemic

The Covid-19 pandemic posed challenges for the psychological care of adolescents, and in particular those in therapeutic groups. Exploring three sessions of a psychoanalytic psychodrama group in early 2021 in a day hospital in Paris, the article considers how the pandemic threatened the intra- and intersubjective relations, and how these dangers were elaborated. The psychodrama group helped the adolescents to create fantasy and symbolisation activities, so that a transitional space could be restored.

[[RunningHead]]

Marie Langer, Psychoanalysis and Science Fiction: A Yearning for Change

Francisco Pizarro Obaid and Rodrigo De la Fabián

Rodrigo De la Fabián

#Grajales 1898, Santiago, Chile
francisco.pizarro@udp.cl
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-8344-4971

Santiago, Chile
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2374-6289

Rodrigo De la Fabián  

Marie Langer, psychoanalysis, science fiction, change

Marie Langer (1910–1987) has been recognised as a key psychoanalyst in the development of Argentine and Latin American psychoanalysis. However, in contrast to the many works devoted to exploring her life trajectory or the varied research on her contribution to institutional or theoretical psychoanalytic discussion, studies on her connection with science fiction (SF) have been anecdotal. Since the mid-fifties, Langer found SF to be a source of theoretical-clinical reflection on psychoanalysis, and a field in which to exercise her creativity by writing SF stories. This article proposes an analysis of Marie Langer's relationship with the SF of her time through an exhaustive review of the literature on this subject. On the one hand, it analyses the interdisciplinary reflections that she established between psychoanalysis and SF, in which she exposed theoretical, clinical, and sociological problems; on the other hand, it examines the literary production of SF that Langer developed in her short stories. This review concluded that her work on SF bears the same hallmark that guided her clinical and institutional relationship with Psychoanalysis: a desire for change.

responseSet.title: On Man Who Lives Once Every Two Times: The Days When I Do Not Exist (2002)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Man Who Lives Once Every Two Times: The Days When I Do Not Exist (2002)

Jan Borowicz

Jan Borowicz

borowiczj@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2847-2686

Jan Borowicz  

Unrepresented mental states, psychic void, autism

The essay delineates a psychic phenomenon of incapacity to remain alive, as depicted in Jean-Charles Fitoussi’s movie The Days When I Do Not Exist (2002). The film introduces a provocative thought experiment, illustrating the life of a man who vanishes every other day, only to reappear at precisely the same location twenty-four hours later. The author contends that this narrative serves as a poignant manifestation of the inability to metabolize emotions, thoughts, and memories, leading to their continual evacuation and ultimate loss. The recurrent disappearance of the protagonist diminishes his ability to feel alive, even upon his reentry into the world. This fragile psyche undergoes a potential transformation when the man falls in love, giving rise to the hope that the relationship with another human being may facilitate the integration of both the represented and unrepresented parts of his mind. An analysis of The Days When I Do Not Exist connects the cinematographic structure and the narrative plot to illustrate unrepresented states of severely damaged minds, which are under constant threat of dissolution.

responseSet.title: In Memory of Harold P. Blum, MD (1929–2024)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Memory of Harold P. Blum, MD (1929–2024)

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam

New Haven, CT, USA
rosemary.balsam@yale.edu

Rosemary Balsam  

Harold Phillip Blum, as anyone who crossed paths with him knows, was a brilliant man, wide-ranging, perceptive, elegant and warm, sociable, loving, family-minded, very generous to younger colleagues and utterly devoted to our field. He died peacefully, of complications after a hip fracture, with his family at his bedside, at age 94. His last words were to his beloved wife of 71 years, Elsa. He said they’d had a great marriage.

Born in 1929, Harold grew up in Brooklyn, New York, the middle child of Yiddish-speaking parents from Eastern Europe, who prized education. Harold did not disappoint. He went early to Cornell University, and there he met his future wife, and married her in 1952. He got his medical degree from Boston University, and after an internship, spent two years in the Navy. In 1957 he moved to Long Island, New York, and went into private practice of psychoanalysis. His analytic training was through Downstate Medical Center in Brooklyn, in the Psychoanalytic Association of New York. His early promise blossomed. Harold has been the Vice-President of the International Psychoanalytic Association (IPA); Executive Director of the Sigmund Freud Archives of the Library of Congress (USA); President of the Psychoanalytic Research and Development Fund of the American Psychoanalytic Association; and Editor-in-Chief of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association. He wrote over 200 papers and 10 books and taught locally, nationally and intercontinentally. In 1976 he was the inaugural winner of the Margaret Mahler Prize for distinguished contributions to child psychoanalysis, and in 1996 he was one of the inaugural winners of the Sigourney Award, a highly prestigious award in the psychoanalytic profession.

He was still valuably contributing in his 90s, as a senior member of the North American Committee of the International Regional Encyclopedic Dictionary (IRED). In each capacity, his leadership has shifted our field in major ways, pushing boundaries towards openness of thinking, pluralism and diversity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Avoiding a Repetition of Past Mistakes: Response to Gozlan’s Critique of “Do We Want to Know?”

Roberto D'Angelo

Roberto D'Angelo

Los Angeles, CA, USA
admin@robertodangelo.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2929-3831

Roberto D'Angelo  

D'Angelo, the author of “Do We Want To Know” responds to Gozlan's critique of his paper. He argues that Gozlan recruits psychoanalytic elitism to discredit his work, rather than engaging with the paper's central concerns. The first issue that Gozlan avoids addressing is the misuse of academic writing to discredit those with whom we disagree, extensively documented in “Do We Want To Know”. The second is the refusal to acknowledge the weak evidence base for gender-affirming interventions for youth increasingly embraced by the prevailing psychoanalytic zeitgeist. D'Angelo suggests that this is a repetition of psychoanalysis' rejection of empirical science, which in the past has led to patient harm and which has contributed to the increasing marginalisation of our profession.
