responseSet.title: Response to Parsons: Correspondence Concerning the Psychoanalytic Controversies Section on the Israel–Palestine Conflict (Issue 1, 2025)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Parsons: Correspondence Concerning the Psychoanalytic Controversies Section on the Israel–Palestine Conflict (Issue 1, 2025)

Shmuel Erlich

Shmuel Erlich

Tel Aviv, Israel
shmuelerlich@gmail.com

Jerusalem, Israel

Shmuel Erlich  

Dear Editor,

I welcome Michael Parsons’ disagreement with my paper as it highlights the core dilemma I raised – and how it becomes misrepresented, confused, and obscured when couched in psychoanalytic-sounding language.

To begin, Parsons misrepresents me as saying that “the only question of psychoanalytic relevance is why discussion of the conflict is biased … against Israel” (my emphasis). He totally ignores my point that what is psychoanalytically relevant is why this particular conflict elicits such disproportionate global emotional upheaval, compared to others of similar or even greater violence.

Parsons’ central claim – that external circumstances affect internal psychic development and well-being – is a well-established view, even a truism. No one has argued against it. It is widely acknowledged and hailed in the non-psychoanalytic world and was acknowledged even by Freud as “complemental series” (e.g. Freud, 1917, 1933, 1939). The question is whether psychoanalysts have anything valuable to contribute to this since our focus and method centre on internal factors. Furthermore, no one, psychoanalysts included, has demonstrated any direct causal relationship between external influences and internal development. Merely to say that he disagrees with the statements that psychoanalytic judgements about external “authorities” [sic] are irrelevant is hardly proof or suggestion as to whether, in what ways, and to what extent such influences are deterministic. Work in Israel and by others with Holocaust survivors, as well as the current enormous amount of work by psychoanalysts and psychotherapists with survivors, relatives, and those not directly affected by the 7 October massacre, demonstrates the tremendous variability of the effects of external trauma on people’s psychological states and resilience.

Parsons quotes Frosh’s assertion that a “failed environment” leaves people with an “internal hole”. Frosh’s evocative description, while moving, is more rhetorical than grounded in psychoanalytic method or clinical evidence. It appeals emotionally but does not advance psychoanalytic understanding. Similarly, presenting Alderdice’s contribution as grounded in psychoanalytic expertise rather than political or mediative insight risks conflating disciplines and overstating psychoanalysis’ remit. The issue is therefore not whether external circumstances are influential, but rather whether as psychoanalysts we have anything valuable to contribute to this beyond vague statements and opinions.

responseSet.title: Response to Moss: Correspondence Concerning the Psychoanalytic Controversies Section on the Israel–Palestine Conflict (issue 1, 2025)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Moss: Correspondence Concerning the Psychoanalytic Controversies Section on the Israel–Palestine Conflict (issue 1, 2025)

Shmuel Erlich

Shmuel Erlich

Tel Aviv, Israel
shmuelerlich@gmail.com

Jerusalem, Israel

Shmuel Erlich  

Dear Editor,

Donald Moss criticizes my use of the first-person singular voice, denouncing it in harsh terms as tyrannical and exclusionary, and as disallowing “otherness” or alternative voices. He has framed his argument in such a way – “epistemologically airtight”, to use his own phrase – that any objection I might raise is presumed to confirm his point. This rhetorical configuration is clever, but it constitutes a distortion: of my original text, of reality, and even of psychoanalytic thought.

First, Moss appears to prefer that I speak in the first-person plural, from within some imagined collective “we”. But by what authority would I be entitled to do so? I cannot speak on behalf of all Israelis, Palestinians, or even psychoanalysts. The invitation to participate in this controversial discussion was clearly addressed to me as an individual, to offer my views, opinions, and insights. Moss is entitled to disagree with those views, but not to invalidate my right to express them by labelling my voice as tyrannical. Ironically, it is Moss who adopts an authoritative (or in his term, tyrannical) tone, beginning his response with “We psychoanalysts”, as if to speak for an undifferentiated collective. In my experience, psychoanalysts are deeply individualistic and often resistant to claims of consensus. Moss's position here risks enacting precisely the exclusionary gesture he attributes to me.

Moss also takes issue with my use of the word “testimony”, linking it to Holocaust survivors and questioning its appropriateness in psychoanalytic discourse. He compares it unfavourably with clinical memory, asking whether patients give us “testimonies” or “memories”. This comparison is not only misleading but also rhetorically manipulative. We are not in the realm of free association or “working through” unconscious material. The events I refer to – bombings, massacres, rocket attacks – are not fantasies but harsh and persistent realities. To describe such experiences from a personal perspective is not to indulge in subjectivity but to provide a grounded account. Patients, too, at times give testimony – not merely to their inner world, but to events that have shattered it. Testimony, as Dori Laub and others (Felman & Laub, 1992) have noted, is a vital part of trauma discourse.

Perverse Sexual Enactment

Steven Groarke

Steven Groarke

London, UK
sgroarke2@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3203-3494

Steven Groarke  

Enactment, fetishism, perverse transference–countertransference, sexuality, scopophilia

This paper is about sexual perversion and its enactment. It is divided into three parts, including, a consideration of William Gillespie's reading of Freud with Klein as the basis on which he formulated a general theory of perversion; a selective reading of more recent works on the psychoanalysis of perversion; and a clinical contribution to the analysis of a case of scopophilia.

responseSet.title: Ontological Psychoanalysis and Types of Experience of Being
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ontological Psychoanalysis and Types of Experience of Being

Leopoldo Fulgencio

Leopoldo Fulgencio

Bloco A, Sala 152, Av. Professor Melo Moraes, n. 1721, São Paulo, SP 05508-030, Brasil
leopoldo.fulgencio@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-5730-7626

Leopoldo Fulgencio  

Experience, self, being, object relations, transference

The discussion about the nature of what is achieved in the analytical process gained a significant contribution with Ogden's proposal, which characterises two modi operandi of the psychoanalytic treatment method (one that seeks understanding, and the other that seeks experiences so that the self may integrate and become more profoundly itself). However, he did not focus on describing the different types of experiences of the self, achievable within the analytical process. This article aims to partially fill this gap by drawing on Winnicott's work, associating experiences of Being with the way he conceives of object relations in the context of the analyst-patient relationship, differentiating and characterising the following experiences: am-ness, when the analyst acts as a subjective object for the patient; am-with, when patient and analyst share transitional phenomena; I-am-different-from and I-am-predicated-on, when the analyst is understood as an external object; and I-am-finite, as an experience, perceived or apperceived, by the self in relation to itself, the other, and the world over time. This understanding can broaden the comprehension of the various ways of experiencing and managing transference within the analytical process.

responseSet.title: “What Baby?”: Creative Disavowal During Second Pregnancy – A Protective Parallel World
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“What Baby?”: Creative Disavowal During Second Pregnancy – A Protective Parallel World

Alejandra Perez

Alejandra Perez

Byron House, 112a Shirland Road, Maida Vale, London, W9 2BT, UK
Alejandra.Perez@19bloomsburysquare.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3330-1152

Alejandra Perez  

Pregnancy, creative disavowal, somatic, reverie, intermediate space

Pregnancy and early motherhood are periods of great psychic demands, in part, due to the various physical changes the mother faces. Women may have a wide range of experiences during this time due to various factors: their early childhood, internal objects, current relationships and environment, as well as their physical state. This paper will explore the experiences of three women, who before starting analysis, had suffered extreme and prolonged physical difficulties in their first pregnancies or post-partum periods, which gave rise to claustrophobic panic and feelings of disintegration. Once in analysis, the three women each went on to have a second baby. Each mother navigated her second pregnancy through a complex process of disavowal of her unborn baby, which I have termed ‘creative disavowal' as it allowed an intermediate area which protected them and their baby from the violent bodily experiences, primitive anxieties, and hatred that they felt, as well as opened space for more passionate feelings to unfold and take shape. The analytic work in each case involved giving meaning to the mother’s somatic experiences, and containing her intense hostile and primitive unconscious phantasies, whilst also creating space for the psychological or imagined baby to be born and held.

responseSet.title: Time and Timelessness in Psychoanalysis: An Introduction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time and Timelessness in Psychoanalysis: An Introduction

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

London, UK
c.bronstein@btinternet.com

London, UK

Catalina Bronstein  

“At a certain point, the past becomes timeless. It’s not a problem of nostalgia, but the past freezes, there is a kind of timelessness”. Modiano 1923 (my translation)

Time, memory, the uncanny, après-coup, Nachträglichkeit, après-coup, deferred action.

Time is untouchable and unreachable. It is the background to our experiences and memories of the past, our phantasies and desires for the future, and our lived present. It is forever shifting in our senses and perspectives. At times, it becomes fixed, submerging us in a timeless world that never changes, of fixed memories and recollection of traumatic events; at others, its plasticity and “elasticity” translate into a vertiginous experience (Bronstein 2024). It makes us aware of our mortality, although we can cancel this out by replacing it with a belief in our immortality. It can possess a linear quality, yet it can also evoke a sense of circularity. Our perception of time can change dramatically according to our state of mind. Time marks the separation and separateness from the object, as well as growth and death. However, the interplay of conscious and unconscious phantasies and anxieties, as well as the timelessness of the unconscious – where time does not exist – can present a different reality in our imagination, memories, and dreams, where the dead may reappear or we remain infants. The relationship we establish with “objective” time can feel vastly different from one person to another. We might try to search for explanations and reasons for our conflicts in our history, but we might neglect the idea that we are subjected to the impact of our unconscious phantasies or to what Pontalis sees as the living and inexhaustible source of the infantile (Pontalis 1997).

Understanding this subject is far from easy. We meet the necessity of understanding the development and meaning of what is subjectively felt and can be phenomenologically described, as well as to try to establish the meta-psychological foundations of psychic functioning, from topographical and structural perspectives. Moreover, we need to consider that all experiences involve a temporal dimension, as we exist “in time”. This includes the possibility of their representation. As Stern described, “temporal contours provide the backbone that permits affective experiences to be represented” (Stern 1998, 84). From all this, we can see the intrinsic difficulty analysts face when trying to address a particular subject, such as “time”, as historical-genetic approaches and objective reality come together and sometimes enter into conflict with the understanding of psychic reality.

responseSet.title: Future Tense: Permacrisis, Falling Out of the World, and Our Existential Moment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Future Tense: Permacrisis, Falling Out of the World, and Our Existential Moment

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

Boston, USA
alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

Permacrisis, the uncanny, existential shock, Après-coup, Being-in-the-World, entangled time (past-presentfuture), falling out of the world, future tense

In the spirit of Loewald's synthesis of psychoanalysis and the entwined, temporal–spatial dimensions of being-in-the-world, this paper will explore our historical moment at the cusp of a precarious future. Indeed, we are at the cusp of a great battle for ownership of the future. Building on my previous work, “Falling out of the world”, here we will explore existential dislocation, the uncanny, the loss of our taken-for-granted, everyday world – and the longing to find our way back home.

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand

Aix-en-Provence, France
marie.anne.lenormand@gmail.com

Marie Lenormand  

In his latest paper published in the IJP, Thomas Ogden explores the vital necessity of inventing psychoanalysis with every new patient. This is, of course, not a new idea. As early as 1979, Lacan had already declared: “it’s really annoying that every psychoanalyst should be forced – because they have to – to reinvent psychoanalysis”. Indeed, this classic issue lays bare a deep, fundamental question, regarding the very foundations of our practice – and its potential collapse.

According to me, however, Ogden’s views on this issue represent a mortal danger for psychoanalysis.

He begins with a number of valid and compelling points. For example, although this opens up significant problems, he rightly observes that “there is no ‘standard technique’” (5) in psychoanalysis. One can also agree with his assertion that “becoming a psychoanalyst is not a distinction conferred by a psychoanalytic training program”, or with his claim that “a graduate of a psychoanalytic institute has successfully completed the requirements of that training program, but a psychoanalytic institute does not hold the power to confer to the graduate the identity of a psychoanalyst” (1). On this basis, we can agree with him that the specificity of psychoanalysis (“What does it mean to invent psychoanalysis with each patient?”) leads to a number of essential questions.

Despite the relevance of these questions, Ogden’s argument strikes me as problematic. In my view, the notion of a “psychoanalytic identity” – which I see as the cornerstone of his argument – risks undermining the very principles that psychoanalysis is built upon. He writes: “I am practising psychoanalysis because I am a psychoanalyst: that is who I am, how I think, what I do. I am incapable of doing anything else when working with a patient. It would not be me” (3). To my mind, this central assertion is both theoretically and clinically troubling.

Theoretically speaking, the identity of the analyst as analyst cannot serve as a safeguard against deviations from analytic standards, nor can it act as a compass for one’s style or practice. In fact, there is no such thing as an analyst’s identity. This is arguably one of the most logical consequences of the invention of psychoanalysis itself. Why?

responseSet.title: Response to Marie Lenormand
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Marie Lenormand

Thomas H. Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden

thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas H. Ogden  

I would like to thank Dr Lenormand for her thoughtful response to my paper. I am pleased that the paper has been an occasion for fresh consideration of what is fundamental to the theory and practice of psychoanalysis.

In her letter, Dr. Lenormand presents a number of ideas; I will briefly respond to three. The first is the idea that I have “stripped” psychoanalysis of its most fundamental concept, the concept of the unconscious. To my mind, I am not “taking away” the concept of the unconscious, I am reconceiving it. The unconscious is not an entity that has a “place” beneath the conscious mind, it is a quality of thinking, a quality of consciousness. I find apt Merleau-Ponty’s description (in The Phenomenology of Perception) of the unconscious as the “atmosphere” of the mind, an atmosphere pregnant with the known but not yet known.

The second assertion concerns the idea that the “identity” of the analyst is a “specious” notion. I view the identity of the analyst as an experience of self that is constantly evolving. Each session the analyst spends with a patient contributes to that growth. The analyst over time becomes increasingly skilled at creating with the patient a new jointly constructed consciousness with which they dream the session together. The identity of the analyst emerges in the form of his or her unique way of being with his or her patients. It is this identity that the analyst relies upon as he or she creates an analysis with each patient.

The third assertion holds that I am abandoning the central tenets of psychoanalysis in the clinical examples I present. The idea that these illustrations all involve “transgressions” is precisely the idea for which I am trying to offer an alternative way of thinking. To my mind, the analyst must not be a slave to the “rules” and instead create a responsible, flexible style in which he or she is able to be human and humane in his or her practice of psychoanalysis.

Disclosure Statement

No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To the Editors: A Response to “The Phenomenology of Teleanalysis”, and Prof. Lemma’s “What We Don’t Talk About Enough When We Talk About Teleanalysis” by the Author of the Study

Nicolle Zapien

Nicolle Zapien

drzapien@drzapien.com

Nicolle Zapien  

To the Editors,

I am delighted that the findings of my research study “The Phenomenology of Teleanalysis” are being published in the IJP to be shared with all of its readership. I am honoured to have Dr Reith introduce the study and to have Prof. Lemma comment on the study, in her response titled “What We Don’t Talk About Enough When We Talk About Teleanalysis” (Lemma, 2025). With her thoughtful consideration of whether teleanalysis can provide for an analytic process and further the ethical risks of teleanalysis, I think she gets to the heart of what I intended for us to explore by doing the research in the first place, but I also want to invite us to consider a few more issues.

Firstly, I am grateful to Prof. Lemma in her approach to her comments, because it is important on such matters that people engage research, stay close to the findings, and take account of and responsibility for the interpretations thereof, and Prof. Lemma has done just that. No doubt others will interpret the findings of this study differently than Prof. Lemma, and this is one of the many benefits of a descriptive study – it allows each of us to interpret and to consider and debate the merits of our interpretations as well as those of others.

Dr Reith notes that this study is both descriptive and truthful in his editor’s introduction, and while I appreciate his complimentary evaluation, I am not sure I can (or any phenomenologist would) claim to produce a “truthful” account of anything, instead leaving it to the reader to play with the unsaturated descriptive constituents to see how they can be used to stimulate thinking. He points to the need for more research (I would agree) and suggests that some of the qualitative descriptive research findings from this study may provide the impetus for additional research (I agree with this also).

In recent years I have heard or read many senior analysts’ opinions on the value of teleanalytic work and its pitfalls, often not derived from research findings. Most of these arguments seem to be rooted in theoretical differences, personal preferences or cultural norms where the analyst practices. There is also a real concern for the development and growth of psychoanalysis through teleanalysis in particular areas of the world and for new generations of candidates. I have observed the heated debates at my local institute, at The American Psychoanalytic Association (APsA) and the International Psychoanalytic Association (IPA) about the issues associated with offering teleanalysis or not, teletraining or not, and the implications for the growth of psychoanalysis and its availability to more candidates and patients. I do hope that this research study doesn’t only get used in these polarizing debates. To me, the COVID-19 pandemic afforded us a giant opportunity to really think about the theory and practice of psychoanalysis in both in person and teleanalytic formats and to question what makes an analysis special and what is important to preserve, whether the format is online or in person or 1058

[[RunningHead]]

Knowing What Psychoanalysts Do and Doing What Psychoanalysts Know, by David Tuckett, Elizabeth Allison, Olivier Bonard, Georg J. Bruns, Anna L. Christopoulos, Michael Diercks, Eike Hinze, Marinella Linardos, and Michael Šebek. Assisted by Abbot Bronstein and Marie Rudden, Lanham, Maryland, Rowman & Littlefield, 2024, 1–286 pp., £46, ISBN:9781538188101

Louise Gyler

Louise Gyler

louise.gyler@bigpond.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-2132-6934

Louise Gyler  

The title Knowing What Psychoanalysts Do and Doing What Psychoanalysts Know includes the grammatically ambiguous “doing” and “knowing” – are they gerunds or present participles – but regardless of the correct categorisation, these verb-nouns attempt to capture and name the action of psychoanalysts and their relationship to praxis and knowledge. The title resonates with the ambiguous grammar of psychoanalysis itself, residing in the tension between broadly defined science and art. The study described in this volume foregrounds the complexity of our discipline and the two-way relationship between theoretical knowledge and praxis. Central in the story of this relationship is the concept of the unconscious, always challenging and unseating what we believe we know. How do we describe psychoanalytic practice and remain faithful to the enormity of the implications of the notion of the unconscious?

Situated in the German idealist tradition, psychoanalysis shares epistemological commitments that data is selected, and explanations constructed, according to unacknowledged and implicit values and assumptions. As Tuckett et al. remind us, Freud’s concept of knowledge went “beyond the usual, empirical-positivist concepts in cognitive science” (118). Given their impressively demonstrated commitment to Freud scholarship in this volume, their investigation is complex and ambitious. Knowing What Psychoanalysts Do and Doing What Psychoanalysts Know explores two central questions: (1) what psychoanalysts know, which implicates epistemological questions about how we can know; and (2) knowing about the activities/actions a psychoanalyst engages in when they do psychoanalysis – that is, what they do with what they know.

These questions are complicated by the understanding that psychoanalytic concepts are elastic and that clinicians inevitably hold implicit and private formulations that contrast with their public and explicit theories (see Sandler 1983). For example, we are all familiar with analysts who self-identify as working in a particular theoretical model but who present clinical material at odds with the publicly defined technique of that theory. That is, theory, clinical practice and technique have a tenuous relationship, leading to many competing theoretical and technical frameworks being developed and often jealously advocated for. Nevertheless, the psychoanalyst requires a conceptual framework to select and organise the phenomena observed and the affects experienced in the clinical encounter. In this context, it is not surprising that Canestri wrote: “We believe that there has not been as much diligence in confronting the reality of our clinical practice, that is, what it really is, and not what we say it is or what we would like it to be” (Canestri 2012a, emphasis in the original). Tuckett et al. take on this challenge by both collecting thick descriptions of clinical practice and developing an overarching conceptual framework that attempts to transcend the various partisan theoretical and technical commitments.

[[RunningHead]]

Oedipal Experiences in Same-Sex Families. English translation, by Yifat Eitan-Persico, translated by Ilana Goldberg, Routledge, Oxon, 2024, pp. 285, £34.99 (paperback), ISBN 978-1-032-66329-6

Jayne Hankinson

Jayne Hankinson

London, UK
j.hankinson1@btinternet.com

Jayne Hankinson  

This admirable and well-researched book is structured into four parts, three of which tackle the following – “What does Oedipal development have to do with the parents’ sex, anyway?”; “What does genetics have to do with kinship, anyway?”; and “What does procreation have to do with parental coitus, anyway?” The fourth part is an afterword tackling the questions “Oedipus for everyone?” and then “How to work with same-sex families”.

The book aims to update the Oedipus complex for a contemporary audience and explore its implications for our understanding of, and the practical experiences of working with, queer families.

Dr Yifat Eitan-Persico’s use of questions to structure her book tries to build a bridge between the psychoanalytic and empirical fields (26) and invites the reader to challenge their (perhaps) accepted heteronormative and heterosexual views of fundamental aspects of psychoanalytic theory, namely:

… Freud[’s] … primal phantasies are the seduction phantasy (the Oedipal phantasy), the castration phantasy, and the phantasy of witnessing parental coitus (the primal scene) … all … share the child’s preoccupation with origins: “In the primal scene, it is the origin of the subject that is represented; in seduction phantasies … it is the origin or emergence of sexuality; in castration phantasies, the origin of the distinction between the sexes”. (7–8)

Eitan-Persico embarks on a wide-reaching and exhaustive research project, spanning several years, to investigate these phantasies in 33 children between the ages of four and six, who have been planned and cared for within same-sex relationships. Her specific focus was on those same-sex parents who were raising a child born with the assistance of gamete donation. This entailed interviewing the parents together and individually, and interviewing and presenting cards to the child designed to elicit curiosity and stories related to their family configuration. In this way the reader is able to follow the path that Eitan-Persico is taking, as she “ … attempt[s] to present the interpretive process openly, allowing room for the reader’s own judgement” (184).

[[RunningHead]]

The Indian Jungle: Psychoanalysis and Non-Western Civilizations, by Sudhir Kakar, 2024, Karnac Books, Oxfordshire, UK, 150 pp, ₤17.99 (paperback), ISBN 13: 9781915565204; ISBN 10: 1915565200

Gurmeet Kanwal

Gurmeet Kanwal

New York, USA
gkanwalmd@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9870-0544

New York, USA

Gurmeet Kanwal  

In September 2023, in an email to me, Kakar described himself as working on, “the role of cultural imagination in the psyche and limitations of psychoanalysis because the cultural imagination underlying its models and theories is almost solely Western” (personal communication). His final book, The Indian Jungle: Psychoanalysis and Non-Western Civilizations, a compilation of five of his papers, all updated from previous publications, is a result of this preoccupation – an examination of the “terroir” in which Indian psychology, as well as the work of Indian psychoanalysts, grows.

Kakar’s psychoanalytic career and personal interests moved increasingly from looking at culture and behavior through an understanding of the Unconscious, to understanding the Unconscious by looking at the products of cultural imagination. This is evident in his gradual move from non-fictional psychoanalytic scholarship to works of fiction addressing everything from art to desire, ecstasy, love, mysticism, as well as colonial history, the last in a novel titled The Kipling File. That is a novel about a fictional Rudyard Kipling and it explains the cover of this book, The Indian Jungle (depicting a snarling Bengal tiger). It is also relevant to understanding Kakar’s approach to psychoanalysis via cultural imagination.

To understand the link between these two books, and why Kakar chose the cover he did, one has to step two generations back into Indian history during the time of India as a colony of the British Empire. Without that context one also cannot properly appreciate Freud’s letter to Rolland (which appears as a quote headlining the front matter of Kakar’s book) in which Freud talks about his difficulty in penetrating “the Indian jungle,” and from which the title of this book seems to be taken. It is as if Kakar is asking us to ponder, which culture’s imagination is reflected in the cover and title of this book? These, as well as the imagery of Freud’s letter, are actually a product of the cultural imagination of the West, about an India from the late nineteenth century to the mid-twentieth century.

[[RunningHead]]

New Psychoanalytic Readings of Shakespeare: Cool Reason and Seething Brains, edited by James Newlin and James W. Stone, New York and London: Routledge, 2024, 250 pp., £114.75 (hbk), £35.99 (ebk), ISBN 987-1-032-30829-6 (bbk), 987-1-032-30830-2 (pbk), 987-1-003-30689-4 (ebk)

Meg Harris Williams

Meg Harris Williams

meg@artlit.info
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-5759-4151

Meg Harris Williams  

The subtitle of this collection of new essays on Shakespeare, taken from Theseus’s speech about the nature of imagination in A Midsummer Night’s Dream, refers to the traditional dichotomy of science (cool reason) and art (seething emotion) and indicates that the book seeks to marry the two, as does Bottom himself. Psychoanalysis itself is these days generally considered an art–science so it is a fitting umbrella under which to gather a variety of psychoanalytically oriented essays, although not a new approach, any more than it is only “recent conversations” in both Shakespeare studies and psychoanalytic literature that “concern identity” (8). Identity – the peculiarity of human nature in its individual and group manifestations – has always been the focus and rationale behind both psychoanalysis and literature, and the reason for literature’s existence and appeal to readers. Packed theatres show no signs of interest abating, and long may the conversations continue.

Papers published here were presented at a conference in the early stages of the Covid-19 pandemic. The event was brought to an abrupt conclusion when the co-organiser Christian A. Smith (to whom the volume is dedicated) called out “Can’t you see the world is burning?” The editors have noted how the pandemic became a relevant context for structuring the volume according to themes such as tombs, ghosts, sacrifice, and mourning. It also highlights the perennial question of the usefulness of psychoanalysis. Is it just a luxury means for the neurotic bourgeoisie to evacuate their anxieties, or does it have any constructive role in the wider world? Perhaps the world has always been burning and the richer world turned a blind eye, but trauma has always been part of the human condition. Other than entertainment value, of what use is Shakespeare to psychoanalysis and the world at large? Is it possible to have a genuinely interdisciplinary dialogue about the world’s or the individual’s states of mind?

The editors wish to make structural as well as thematic links, and point out how in higher education, literature teachers are “on the front line of the student mental health crisis”, and that whilst not claiming the same expertise as professional counsellors, “the fact remains that working with students experiencing crisis is part of our job” and psychoanalysis offers a “model for focused, sympathetic listening” especially in the face of a reduction in financial support for the humanities where economic value is less measurable than in sciences and technology (10). It is implied here that the knowledge embodied in literature itself is an important resource in enabling the teacher to determine and help contain the anxieties of the student. 1083

responseSet.title: At the Meniscus of Self-Understanding: Rethinking the Examined Life in Technogenetic Times
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

At the Meniscus of Self-Understanding: Rethinking the Examined Life in Technogenetic Times

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
alemma@mac.com

London, UK

Alessandra Lemma  

Adolescence, artificial intelligence, identity, self, psychic structure, technology

Human development unfolds within a psychic ecology increasingly shaped by AI: we have entered technogenetic times. No longer merely a tool, AI structures how we understand ourselves and relate to others. In AI-mediated contexts, self-exploration often remains superficial, particularly among adolescents, eclipsing the messy, non-linear process of self-discovery, which depends on the capacity to tolerate uncertainty. I propose the metaphor of the meniscus - the tense, curved surface where liquid clings to the edge of its container - to describe a defensive psychic structure emerging in this context. It captures my clinical experience with adolescents who engage intensively with AI, where the self, like a liquid, adheres to the surface of its container to maintain its form. However, it does not allow penetration or integration. Just as water molecules cling to the container’s wall to form a meniscus, an adolescent may cling to the digital interface or AI feedback as an external ‘skin’ that contains the self. This results in a one-dimensional self, held together at a surface level, preventing psychic integration. In the consulting room, the analyst’s task becomes one of (re)-establishing psychic three-dimensionality by “claiming” the patient through the here-and-now transference.

responseSet.title: Voice, Body-Self and Trauma in the Analytic Space
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Voice, Body-Self and Trauma in the Analytic Space

Sebastian Leikert

Sebastian Leikert

Germany
s.leikert@web.de

Heidelberg, Germany

Sebastian Leikert  

Voice, body-self, trauma, unconscious, symbolization disorder

The author investigates voice and body-self in the analytic space. A look at the role of music and voice in Stone Age ritual, being the core of human culture, and at the role of the mother's voice, being the unborn child's first encounter with the Other, shows the influence of the voice on psychic life. Beyond the symbolic discourse, voice and body-self organize the exchange of emotions. This level becomes crucial when a symbolization disorder dominates the encounter as a result of traumatization. The analytic couple is not limited to a focus on words and images. Both analyst and patient have access to the perception of their body-self; moreover, the sound of the voice proves to be isomorphic to the states of the body-self and provides vocal information. In the analytic space a traumatic inscription in the body-self (encapsulated body engram) can be focused on. Somatic narration describes a way to work through such an engram. Here, the patient is invited to describe his or her body perceptions and thus the disorganized tensions of traumatization can unfold in the analytic space. This enables a cathartic process of overcoming the destructive qualities of traumatic memory. A case report illustrates the method.

responseSet.title: Sensoriality and Thought. In Development and in Psychopathology
responseSet.authorMast: Franco De Masi and  Gabriella Giustino
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sensoriality and Thought. In Development and in Psychopathology

Franco De Masi and Gabriella Giustino

Gabriella Giustino

Milano, Italy
gabriella.giustino3@gmail.com

Gabriella Giustino  

Sensoriality, development, trauma

This paper addresses certain aspects of the role of sensoriality in psychic functioning and, in particular, seeks to compare child sensoriality – a natural component of development that precedes the ability to think – and the perceptual-sensorial distortion that occurs in certain pathological conditions. In the course of severe psychic disorders such as psychosis, and in other respects in post-traumatic conditions, the mind is invaded by sensorial perceptions that block its functioning as an organ of knowledge. In the case of trauma, the intrusive mental phenomena are more limited and related to untransformed traumatic anxiety. Instead, the psychotic transformation of the mind is most radical; it develops into psychic retreat that shatters the ability to think and perceive psychic reality. In this instance, the mind’s sensorial mode of functioning is so extreme that these patients are immersed in a newly created sensorial reality; they do not think but rather see their own thoughts or perceive sounds or voices that do not exist.

responseSet.title: Confidentiality, Anonymisation and their Digital Discontents
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Confidentiality, Anonymisation and their Digital Discontents

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

London, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

Elizabeth Allison  

confidentiality, publishing, history of psychoanalysis, Freud

Scientific communication about clinical work is complicated for psychoanalysts by the requirement to protect confidentiality, the potentially disruptive effect that seeking consent may have on the treatment, and questions about whether informed consent is possible if the unconscious is taken seriously. For these reasons, the balance of opinion has tended to favour disguise over consent, but in the digital age and with the advent of AI doubts have arisen about whether it is possible to achieve a level of disguise that would prevent any third party from recognising the patient. The papers in this Psychoanalytic Controversies section by John Churcher, Denia Barrett, Aden Evens and Sarah Ackerman, Vittorio Lingiardi and Mariana Liotti, and David Tuckett explore different aspects of the question of whether in the digital age effective anonymisation is possible.

responseSet.title: Is Anonymisation Possible?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is Anonymisation Possible?

John Churcher

John Churcher

Manchester, UK
churcher@aulos.co.uk

John Churcher  

Anonymisation, confidentiality, uncertainty

In today’s world of rapidly evolving digital technology, guaranteeing the anonymity of a psychoanalytic patient in a published paper is very much harder than it may seem. Anonymisation in publishing is part of a wider problem of psychoanalytic confidentiality. Identifying details can be changed but information relating to external reality may still be present in the text. The risk of deanonymisation often cannot be estimated and is instead subject to radical uncertainty. The paper considers various sources of this radical uncertainty. Anonymisation of a patient may need to remain effective for decades. The conditions for ensuring this are exacting and rarely likely to be met in practice. Possible consequences of abandoning belief in the safety of anonymisation are briefly considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What a Tangled WEB we Weave: Straight Talk about Anonymising Clinical Writing about Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis

Denia Barrett

Denia Barrett

30 North Michigan Avenue, Chicago, Illinois 60602, USA
deniabarrett@gmail.com

Denia Barrett  

anonymisation, confidentiality, ethics, clinical writing, child and adolescent psychoanalysis

This paper explores the ethical challenges child and adolescent psychoanalysts encounter in trying to preserve the confidentiality of their patients and their families while still sharing what they have learned from this work by writing about it for professional colleagues. These challenges are made even more difficult in our digital age, when almost anything one has written can be discovered. An example of an adult who found papers written by an analyst seen in childhood serves as a cautionary tale.

responseSet.title: Breadth Psychology
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Breadth Psychology

Aden Evens and Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

Hanover, NH, USA

Boston, MA, USA
53 S. Main St, Suite 311, Hanover, NH 03755, USA
ackerman@post.harvard.edu

Sarah Ackerman  

Confidentiality, artificial intelligence, ethics

Freudian psychoanalysis holds a conception of personhood in which patients are divided by a conflict between their outward presentation and their hidden, inner turmoil. The Freudian analyst too is split, occupying a position within the patient's internal dialogue while also listening in from outside of it. Addressing the analyst as both self and other, the patient's confidentiality is necessarily preserved but also inherently violated. As digital technologies reshape psychic structure, this paradox in confidentiality, constitutive of the analytic situation, may no longer arise. Personhood in the digital age is arguably less grounded in the patient's internality and instead diffused into a distributed, online self. How should our analytic task—including the preservation of confidentiality—be altered for patients who construct their selves in external digital networks? Whether the patients' growing disregard for privacy and evacuation of innerness is a defensive resistance or a tectonic shift in the structure of the psyche, the analyst must remain committed to an ethics in practice, as abstinence, neutrality, and anonymity (confidence) are not only gestures of professional respect for the analysand but in situ features of a successful treatment, features whose intransigence safeguards the fundamental rule and so makes it possible to vary other aspects of technique to discover what complementary subject position best frees the patient's desire, regardless of the shape of her psyche (Report on Confidentiality, 2018, p. 6).

responseSet.title: As Fragments of a Vessel: On Anonymisation and the Ethics of Clinical Case Writing
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

As Fragments of a Vessel: On Anonymisation and the Ethics of Clinical Case Writing

Vittorio Lingiardi and Marianna Liotti

Marianna Liotti

Via dei Marsi 75, 00185 Roma, Italy
vittorio.lingiardi@uniroma1.it
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1298-3935

Rome, Italy

Rome, Italy

Marianna Liotti  

Anonymisation, case-writing, LGBTQIA+, epistemic injustice, epistemic trust, clinical truth

Case-writing is always poised between the wish to convey clinical truth and the duty to protect the patient’s privacy. In the digital age, where clinical material circulates widely and remains permanently accessible, this tension has become more acute. We argue that anonymisation should not be reduced to the concealment of details regarding our patients but understood as a transformation process akin to mentalisation: a way of reshaping raw experience into a narrative that can be shared without falsifying the therapeutic encounter. Preserving clinical truth requires placing the therapeutic alliance and the patient’s trust at the centre of the writing process. Yet we must also remember that anonymisation is never neutral. For patients belonging to minoritised communities, such as LGBTQIA+ individuals, alterations intended to protect privacy may risk reproducing forms of misrecognition, thereby committing epistemic injustice. We propose that case-writing must be guided by a relational ethic: cultivating humility, openness, and disciplined curiosity to sustain trust and safeguard the therapeutic alliance, while distributing responsibility for confidentiality across the psychotherapeutic community as a whole.

responseSet.title: We Must Publish Material from Our Sessions, So how do We do It Safely?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

We Must Publish Material from Our Sessions, So how do We do It Safely?

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK
d.tuckett@ucl.ac.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6275-2289

David Tuckett  

Anonymisation, clinical material, publishing

This paper discusses whether the Internet and related technologies that we can imagine in future may have made it unsafe for psychoanalysts to publish clinical material in an anonymised form. The author notes, first, that algorithms work with identifiers of various sorts to tie information subjects together across space and time. It means that what is an anonymised clinical report today might, in future, potentially be joined with other documents to allow a patient to be re-identified. Discussing the current literature from computer science, the case is made that while there will be risks, if anonymisation is done less than rigorously, they can be removed by a variety of measures, most important of which are both the generalisation and falsification of potential identifiers. Exploring the merits and ethics of this approach, the author argues that psychoanalytic data are not externally valid data but internally consistent subjective data. It means that ensuring external identifiers are absent or distorted in clinical reports to convey the analyst's understanding but to safeguard against jigsaw puzzle re-identification does not de-authenticate them. Indeed, the process of anonymisation, focusing on the status of psychoanalytic data as inherently subjective, should deepen and strengthen rather than reduce their evidential values.

Bernard Reith

Bernard Reith

Boulevard des Philosophes 21, 1205 Genève, Switzerland
dr.bernard.reith@hin.ch

Bernard Reith  

Superego, ethics, countertransference

We are moved to present this paper, chosen by Dana Birksted-Breen for the Key Papers section, before she passed away. There is an important ethical preoccupation running through this complex paper by Francis Pasche and the discussions by Nathalie Zilkha and Vic Sedlak, who all insist on the importance of the psychoanalyst's countertransference work. Dana would have shared this concern.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Francis Pasche’s (1993) Text “From the Ambivalent Superego to the Impersonal Superego”: A Brief Introduction or Towards an Impersonal and Loving Superego**

Nathalie Zilkha

Nathalie Zilkha

nathalie.zilkha@bluewin.ch

Nathalie Zilkha  

Francis Pasche, ego-superego relations, psychoanalytic ethic

This paper revisits Francis Pasche's 1993 seminal essay From the Ambivalent Superego to the Impersonal Superego. Particular emphasis is given to Pasche's view on the specific verticality in the relation between the ego and superego, and on its evolution. Pasche conceptualises the transformation of this relation from primary admiration and idealization towards an impersonal superego, which he describes as apophatic - defined by what it is not. In doing so, Pasche outlines an ethical horizon that is central to his conception of psychoanalysis, and more specifically of the analyst's inner stance. These ideas are discussed more precisely in reference to some of his other papers. Beyond Pasche's concepts, further reflection is given to the relationship between the ego and the superego and to the way it evolves, notably through processes of de-identification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Francis Pasche’s Paper “From the Ambivalent Superego to the Impersonal Superego”.

Vic Sedlak

Vic Sedlak

London, UK
sedlak@blueyonder.co.uk

Vic Sedlak  

Introduction

As Dana Birksted-Breen and Sara Flanders note in their introduction to Reading French Psychoanalysis (2010, 1): “While psychoanalysts all over the world recognize their Freudian origins it is undeniable that psychoanalysis has developed along different lines of interest in different geographical areas due to cultural phenomena and to the influence of important or charismatic figures”. Perhaps this is nowhere clearer than in the different psychoanalytic cultures that have developed in two countries that are geographically very close neighbours in Europe: France and Great Britain. Putting to one side for the moment the influence of figures such as Melanie Klein and Jacques Lacan, psychoanalysis in the two countries has been hugely influenced by the cultural milieux in which it has been located; in France psychoanalysis has traditionally been tied closely to philosophy, while in Great Britain empiricism and reliance on clinical evidence have been predominant in the psychoanalytic discourse.

While I was very pleased to be invited to discuss this paper, I soon found it was a far more difficult task than I had first envisaged. Francis Pasche’s references to philosophical concepts and (until the latter part of the paper) his forgoing a discussion of actual clinical technique made this a difficult read. I frequently had to resort to reference books, colleagues and Google to enable me to understand some of the terms used in his paper. Despite this research and consultation, I am sure that some of Pasche’s subtler points continue to escape me. But I also found the paper of great interest and a stimulus for clarifying my own thoughts about the origins and the development of the superego and the differences between Pasche’s views and my own.

For many years I had been under the impression that the important role of the superego, in the understanding of mental health and pathology, was understated in French psychoanalysis. There are only scattered references to the concept in the comprehensive survey of French psychoanalytic contributions edited by Birksted-Breen, Flanders and Gibeault (2010), and this view has been supported by my informal enquiries amongst colleagues [who have also pointed out some notable but comparatively rare exceptions, e.g. Donnet (1995)]. Here perhaps the influence of Lacan does need to be acknowledged since it is his emphasis on Freud’s writings, prior to the revision that introduced the structural model of the mind, that may have played some part in the comparative neglect of the role of the superego and particularly its early manifestations in the child’s mind.

responseSet.title: Working with Dreams Which Take on a Landmark Significance
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working with Dreams Which Take on a Landmark Significance**

Clara Nemas

Clara Nemas

Buenos Aires, Argentina
claranemas@gmail.com

Clara Nemas  

Dreams, technique of dream interpretation, psychoanalytic atmosphere

This paper examines dreams that become landmarks in analytic work, opening new pathways for understanding a patient's internal world. Through the analysis of a young patient whose sessions oscillated between despair, omnipotence, and intense claustrophobic anxieties, it shows how dreams provided a shared space for the development of imaginative conjectures. A central dream—structured as a film perceived from multiple points of view—revealed the patient's unstable contact-barrier and her confusion between psychotic and non-psychotic modes of experience. The paper shows how this dream provided a perspective from which to observe the mental functioning of the patient, changing the initial atmosphere of despair into a possibility of exploration and thinking, becoming a landmark in the development of the analytic process. A supervisory vignette further illustrates how approaching dreams as a way of understanding the architecture of the mind could transform a negative therapeutic atmosphere into one of curiosity and collaboration.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Online Training: Innovation, Necessity, or the End of Psychoanalysis? Report on the Panel at the International Psychoanalytic Congress, Lisbon, Portugal 2025

Marina de Litvan

Marina de Litvan

Rambla Armenia 3783, ap.1001, Montevideo, Uruguay
marina.altmann@gmail.com

Marina de Litvan  

Online training, online practice, supervision

The title of this panel introduces a question that permeates the presentations, reflecting complex tensions with the question of psychoanalytic training online. The topic was the effect of presence or distance analysis on candidate training. We asked: Does teleanalysis differ from in-person analysis, and if so, how, and how may it affect candidate training?

How will core concepts of analytic education, such as transference, resistance, unconscious functioning, regression, free association, use of countertransference, neutrality, and authenticity fare in the new world of training via teleanalysis?

For some, virtual psychoanalytic training is a necessity – imposed by distance, pandemic, or external factors. They see it as a pragmatic adaptation to preserve continuity. For others, the issue is how digital mediation transforms training, supervision, and transmission.

A third perspective raises concerns about a threat to the integrity of psychoanalytic education, worrying that virtual training compromises analytic training.

The panel surfaced these contemporary conflicts. The three presentations addressed the theme of online training from different experiential perspectives.

The panel proposes examining online training within the context of different psychoanalytic training models: Eitingon, French, and Uruguayan. The experiences presented are based on each model and sometimes began during the pandemic. The Uruguayan model was briefly introduced in the discussion by the Chair.

None of the presentations addressed seminars, even though they are a fundamental pillar. The articulation exists among three interconnected spaces: analysis, supervision, and seminars.

Dominique Suchet discussed supervision and the transition from two to three sessions of the patient’s analysis, as an example of resistance. Her presentation belongs to the French model, which differs from other models not only in the number of weekly sessions but also in how it designs the training process. It requires attention to transferential and

[[RunningHead]]

Differentiation of Dreams Dreamt by the Psychotic and the Non-Psychotic Personalities

Uta Karacaoğlan

Uta Karacaoğlan

55, Köln, Germany
karacaoglan@t-online.de

Uta Karacaoğlan  

Dream, psychosis, psychotic and non-psychotic personalities, body

To what extent are dreams dreamt by the psychotic or non-psychotic parts of the personality? And what significance does this have for the treatment technique? Based on Bion's theories of dream function and the differentiation between psychotic and non-psychotic personalities, these questions are examined. Theoretically, reference is made to the significance of the body and body image with regard to both psychotic structure and dream function. Using a countertransference dream by Winnicott and material from psychoanalytic treatments with patients suffering from psychotic disorders, the above ideas will be discussed.

Poul Rohleder

Poul Rohleder

Colchester, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-5052-0464

London, UK

Poul Rohleder  

Homosexuality, homophobia, melancholia

This paper explores the impact of homophobia, internalised homophobia and heteronormativity on the psychic lives of some gay men, as depicted in the film “All of Us Strangers”. Themes of Oedipal rejection, alienation, melancholia, mourning and loss are explored.

responseSet.title: Injury, Grievance, and Revenge, in the Wrath of Achilles
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Injury, Grievance, and Revenge, in the Wrath of Achilles

John Steiner

John Steiner

63 Cholmley Gardens, London NW6 1AJ, UK
johnsteiner28@gmail.com

John Steiner  

Grievance, revenge, mourning

In this paper I want to trace the complex relationship between grievance and revenge. Both are expressions of the rage that arises in response to feelings of injustice and humiliation, but they differ in the direction that the rage is expressed. In revenge it is outwardly directed, usually as a violent attack, while in grievance the violence is inhibited and the rage is held inwardly in a state of withdrawal. I will use characters and episodes from Richard Holmes' life of Coleridge and from Homer's Iliad to explore this theme, especially with respect to Achilles, whose rage is the chief subject of the Iliad. It is initially expressed as a withdrawal into grievance and subsequently as an active rampage of violent revenge. I will look at the consequences of these different forms of expression of anger and also try to explore what factors enabled the direction of his rage to be so dramatically reversed.

responseSet.title: Inventing Psychoanalysis with Each Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inventing Psychoanalysis with Each Patient

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA
Thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas Ogden  

Becoming a psychoanalyst, Psychoanalytic theory, Psychoanalytic method

The author posits that for an analytic treatment to be alive and effective, the analyst must invent psychoanalysis with each patient. In responding to the question, “What does it mean to invent psychoanalysis with each patient?” the analyst must first ask himself, “What does it mean to become a psychoanalyst?” and “What is it that defines psychoanalysis.” Further, “What is distinctive about the practice of psychoanalysis?”

In responding to these questions, it becomes apparent that what is definitive of psychoanalysis as a discipline and as a clinical practice are matters that each of us must come to on our own, and that the process of becoming a psychoanalyst is a highly personal endeavor that continues through one’s years of practice.

Becoming a psychoanalyst is not a distinction conferred by a psychoanalytic training program. Development of an analytic sensibility is built upon one’s personal analysis, one’s individual study of psychoanalysis, and one’s clinical experience.

The author presents clinical illustrations of some of the ways he, with each of his patients, invents a form of psychoanalysis unique to the two of them.

responseSet.title: From Prenatal Relations to the Constituting of the Self: A Neurobehavioural Perspective on Primary Narcissism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Prenatal Relations to the Constituting of the Self: A Neurobehavioural Perspective on Primary Narcissism

Vittorio Gallese

Vittorio Gallese

Parma, Italy
vittorio.gallese@unipr.it

New York, NY, USA

Vittorio Gallese  

Primary narcissism, self development, embodiment

Building on the centrality of the Narcissus myth and its cultural and psychological implications, the notion of primary narcissism is discussed against the backdrop of neurobehavioural research on the development of the Self. While the concept of primary narcissism emphasises egocentrism, contemporary research suggests a different understanding of the early stages of self-experience. As Freud anticipated, there is a continuum between pre- and post-natal life: early social connections and relationships, beginning in the womb, shape the developing self. This continuum invites the investigation of foetal life to understand the development of a coherent pattern of relationships with the world. The active engagement of foetuses and infants with the outside world is the primum movens of the stages that enable them to live in the world. The quality of postnatal mother-infant relationships and their co-regulation originate in the womb. This suggestion about foetal social life can be seen in part as a development of some of the original insights of psychoanalysis. While emphasizing the importance of narcissism in understanding the Self and its relationship to society, a broader perspective on the early constitutive elements of the Self is needed.

[[RunningHead]]

Responding to the Israel–Palestine Conflict

John Alderdice

John Alderdice

Oxford, UK
john.alderdice@hmc.ox.ac.uk

John Alderdice  

Peace-making, just war, morality and war

The author describes how he first applied psychoanalytical ideas in his political leadership of the Alliance Party of Northern Ireland, particularly in the Irish Peace Process. He then took the lessons learnt from the negotiation of the 1998 Belfast/Good Friday Agreement with him in addressing the conflict in Israel/Palestine. The paper pays particular attention to the moral dilemmas for the governments of the United Kingdom and the United States of America as they try to square the military response of their ally, Israel, to the Hamas attacks on October 7, 2023, with their other international commitments and with the traditional requirements of “just war” theory.

responseSet.title: Challenges to Maintaining the Analytic Frame During the 2023/24 War on Gaza
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Challenges to Maintaining the Analytic Frame During the 2023/24 War on Gaza

M. Davids

M. Davids

4 Primrose Gardens, London NW3 4TJ, UK
fakhrydavids@gmail.com

London, UK

Colchester, UK

M. Davids  

Introduction

I would like to begin with an incident I heard of several months into Israel’s war in Gaza, which followed Hamas’ attacks in Israel on 7 October 2023 and is on-going as I write.

An analysand deeply opposed to Israel’s actions in Gaza, which she saw as excessively brutal, inhumane and barbaric, reported that every time she mentioned the suffering of the women and children of Gaza her analyst would add “and Israel”. This was experienced as a violent intrusion, inserting something alien into her unfolding narrative, which caused her to lose her train of associations – linked, perhaps, with suffering of her own as yet out of reach. Instead, “and Israel” introduced a narrative of competing sufferings, lining patient and analyst up on opposite sides of a political divide. To the patient it felt profoundly wrong for the analyst’s identification with Israel to be allowed to intrude into her analytic space in this way.

This brief vignette does seem to suggest a failure on the analyst’s part to approach her patient’s Gaza material with evenly suspended attention (Freud 1912), a key element of the analytic frame. I would like to suggest that the outbreak of hostilities brought to the fore the analyst’s in-group identification with Israel as part of a warring mindset which her patient’s identification with Gazans brought into the open. This disturbance in the analyst required processing, which her enactment bypassed.

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine produced several examples of in-group identification being mobilised as part of an us–them system in times of war. Mirza and Romanov (2022, 428), writing in the aftermath of the invasion, note that it is “impossible to figure out which Russian psychoanalysts … have been affected” by Putin’s propaganda and were thus hostile towards Ukraine. Sergio Benvenuto (2022), a Western clinical seminar leader, concluded that his Russian group’s failure to disown the invasion meant that they were identified with their country’s aggression, which placed them in opposition to psychoanalysts’ shared “ethical commitment to freedom as foundational” (2). The divide between the two was felt to be so deep as to rule out a psychoanalytic engagement between them, leading to the suspension of the seminar.

responseSet.title: Freud and the Riddle of Time: Some Puzzling Aspects
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and the Riddle of Time: Some Puzzling Aspects

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Frankfurt a.M., Germany
heinzweiss3@outlook.de
weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Heinz Weiss  

In this paper I explore the issue of time in Freud’s writings from different points of view: his linking of time with consciousness, the supposed timelessness of the unconscious, the return of the repressed, the dialectical approach to time in his concepts of bi-temporality and afterwardness, the notion of “transience”, and time as a “feeler”. I will argue that there is no uniformity in Freud’s conception of time, which leaves space for “heterochronicity” and different representations of temporal experience.
